BillingAgreement search_transactions returns empty dict for recurring payment
issue_body:
I am successfully creating and executing billing agreements.  My merchant-facilitator as well as my test-buyer are receiving Notifications in the sandbox saying that the agreement is created and subsequent Notifications that the first recurring payment has been made. 

At this point, I would like to have a daily cron job running to look at my billing agreements and see if any transactions have been made so that I can update my website's userprofile data accordingly.  In order to do this, I am calling billing_agreements.search_transactions() following your sample code provided here on GitHub. Unfortunately, this call is returning an empty dictionary as if there are no transactions for the valid billingagreement.  

I am not sure if the problem is with the search_transactions() function or if there is a problem on the sandbox end. I will note, that in my paypal dashboard I do not see any recurring "transactions" but I do see direct creditcard "Sale" transactions I have made. Also as noted, I DO receive Notifications in the sandbox accounts that the recurring payment has been made so this leads me to believe there should be transactions associated with it.

Downloadable packages are provided through HTTP without providing any verification sum.

Would it be possible to share the SHA256 sum of the packages?

issue_body:
The release of Django REST Framework 3.5.x introduced support for multiple open API standards including OpenAPI/Swagger and CoreAPI.

See: http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/schemas/

Swagger, especially, facilitates the ability to do client code generation. This can lay the groundwork for simplifying the ability to create NSoT clients in other languages. At the very least, the new schema definitions are more robust and will provide a means for better programmatic enumeration of API endpoints for clients.

issue_title: SSH library dependencies no longer supported
issue_body:
The SSHLibrary depends on paramiko, which (used to) depends on PyCrypto.  The latest version of paramiko, 2.0, now depends on Cryptography.  This breaks the SSHLibrary.  When attempting to Login, I'm getting the following error: 

ImportError: dynamic module does not define init function (init_constant_time).

Workaround: Uninstall paramiko 2.0 and cryptography and install paramiko 1.7.5 from source, https://pypi.python.org/pypi/paramiko/1.7.5 (bringing PyCrypto along with it).

cc @perrygeo

The upload API touches AWS S3 services. We should use mocks to unit test this interaction. Initial attempts with moto were not successful due to [this error](https://github.com/spulec/moto/issues/440)

What's the #1 Python library for spatial features? [Fiona](https://github.com/Toblerity/Fiona) :smile:, so this API client should aim to fit that well. Here's how that could go (a document work in progress).

## uploading features to Mapbox

Let's say a Mapbox dataset is among other things a Python [mapping](https://docs.python.org/2/library/stdtypes.html#mapping-types-dict). Adding new features or updating existing features could be done by calling its [update()](https://docs.python.org/2/library/stdtypes.html#dict.update) method.

```python
import fiona
import mapbox

with mapbox.dataset(...) as dst, fiona.open('example.shp') as src:
    dst.update((ftr['id'], ftr) for ftr in src)
```

## fetching a feature from Mapbox

Fetching a feature would be done by using the familiar item getter. It would return a GeoJSON-ish object.

```python
with mapbox.dataset(...) as dst:
    ftr = dst['lolwut']
    # ftr: {'id': 'lolwut', 'type': 'Feature', ...}
```

## deleting a feature from Mapbox

As with a mapping.

```python
with mapbox.dataset(...) as dst:
    del dst['lolwut']
```

issue_title: Package locking is crazy slow for scikit-learn
issue_body:
I'm sorry I don't have a reduced test case for this but this is so crazy slow it's hard to actually debug.

#### Steps to reproduce

1. Clone https://github.com/HearthSim/hearthsim-vagrant
2. Inside that directory, clone https://github.com/HearthSim/HSReplay.net
3. Run `docker-compose build django` (this builds an image based on the python-stretch docker image, which will also install the latest pipenv systemwide, cf. [Dockerfile](https://github.com/HearthSim/hearthsim-vagrant/blob/master/Dockerfile)).
4. Finally, run `docker-compose run django`, which [runs `pipenv install --dev`](https://github.com/HearthSim/hearthsim-vagrant/blob/1ab4c968ff1508176e7710a97691970d3dfa9051/docker-compose.yml#L42)

On linux, this stays stuck at `Locking [packages]` for over 15 minutes, with no output even when run with `--verbose`. Then after ~15 mins, it gives me the full output of what it's been doing for all that time.
When run outside of docker, it still takes a couple of minutes on that step, but at most 1-2 mins. I have a pretty beefy CPU and SSD, so I don't know why it would take this long in the first place.

I also see a lot of `Warning: Error generating hash for ...` in the verbose output, I don't know if that's related.

Any idea? How can I debug this further?

issue_title: lock fails with conditional dependencies on sys_platform
issue_body:
**tldr**: Locking fails when a dependency isn't available on the current platform, even though it's declared conditional on the platform.

##### Background 
I have a machine learning project where development happens on macOS and longer training runs on linux machines with gpus, for which I use tensorflow. Tensorflow uses different packages depending on if you use gpu or not (`tensorflow` vs `tensorflow-gpu`).

The availability for these packages looks like the following:   
`tensorflow==1.6.0`:         linux - :white_check_mark: | macOS :white_check_mark:
`tensorflow-gpu==1.6.0`: linux - :white_check_mark:  |  macOS :x:

In the `Pipfile` the tensorflow requirement is specified as follows:
```
tensorflow = {version = "==1.6.0", sys_platform = "== 'darwin'"}
tensorflow-gpu = {version = "==1.6.0", sys_platform = "!= 'darwin'"}
```
##### Problem
If I create the `Pipfile.lock` on the linux machine, and the run `pipenv sync` with that lockfile on a mac everything installs fine. However, if I try to add a new requirement on a mac (e.g. `pipenv install requests`), which results in pipenv creating another lock, it fails because it can't find the requirement `tensorflow-gpu==1.6.0`.

##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type: macOS Version 10.13.3
1. Python version: 3.5.2
1. Pipenv version: 11.1.3

##### Expected result

```
>> pipenv lock
Locking [dev-packages] dependencies…
Locking [packages] dependencies…
```

Or: to be able to use `pipenv` together with [PEP508](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0508/#environment-markers) dependency specification to do cross-platform development in situations where a specific package is only available on one platform.

issue_title: py_limited_api calculates extension module filename incorrectly
issue_body:
In https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/2a0db96ee5e78a4d555cbd7288029cbd975c5976/setuptools/command/build_ext.py#L113-L116 a suffix of `get_config_var("SO")` is removed from the filename calculated by the superclass; however, the actual suffix that the superclass uses is `get_config_var("EXT_SUFFIX")`: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/a9e7db8845551e5639e1fa8030a0482363501fa2/Lib/distutils/command/build_ext.py#L680. As these two values are the same on many platforms, this problem is often hidden, but where they are not the same (eg. CPython 3.3 on Linux), the generated extension filename is wrong and fails to import (it'll be something like `foo.cpython-33m.abi3.so`).

(Thanks to @dstufft for tracking down the exact misbehaviour causing this)

issue_title: Add additional headers in all requests during multipart upload
issue_body:
About a month ago I've made a pull requests to add support for additional headers in get requests (https://github.com/minio/minio-py/pull/522). I'm using this headers in both, put and get methods, to enable SSE-C in S3 storage. Additional metadata was implemented before for `put_object` and `fput_object`. 

But, as I discovered later, this is not working with objects, large then `MIN_OBJECT_SIZE` (5MiB currently), because `minio` start a multipart upload and transmit this headers only in first request, which initiate this upload. In the same time, when you enable SSE-C in initial multipart request, you need to provide the same headers for all part's request. So I've created PR to add this in `_do_put_multipart_upload`. As additional benefit, I've add it to `_complete_multipart_upload`, to make it look more finished. Last request don't required this headers, but this add ability to transmit [Common Request Headers](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTCommonRequestHeaders.html) which it support.

Here is this PR: https://github.com/minio/minio-py/pull/544

issue_title: Add static_path to the config file
issue_body: Provide the static_path configuration under the app section of the application config file.

The docstring for `HasHeaders` says:
> The headers keys are lowercased, and keys and values are compared in their bytes-encoded forms.

But values aren't actually compared in their bytes-encoded forms.

This is because the header dicts are encoded like this:
```python
dict([(_maybeEncode(k).lower(), _maybeEncode(v)) for k, v in headers.items()])
```
...but `v` is a list so `_maybeEncode` just returns the list unchanged as it is not a string.

`v` must be a list and not a string because.. header values should be lists.. and, `HasHeaders` also does `set(v)` later on.

Does txmongo support nearest read preference? 
I don't see it in the code.
Thanks.

These errors appear on machines that produce messages from the us-west to us-east AWS regions and thus have a higher than normal amount of latency. The problem here is that this situation doesn't cause a crash or stacktrace, but only a hang. That makes it impossible for the monitoring daemon to restart these processes.

I've managed to replicate this issue locally by introducing artificial network latency:

    sudo tc qdisc add dev lo root netem delay 8000ms

When I run a producer under these conditions, it quickly gets to a point where it can no longer produce any messages, and even the main thread is hung.
If the `venv` module is available we should use and prefer that over the `virtualenv` executable, which may not be installed for people using Python 3.3+ (since it is no longer necessary).

issue_title: encapsulate logger as _logger, to imply private
kubernetes apis and models inaccessible through openshift client
issue_body:
I believe it should be possible to access kubernetes apis and models directly through and openshift client instance, but that seems to be broken.

```
>>> from openshift import config
>>> from openshift import client as ocpclient
>>> from kubernetes import client as k8sclient
>>> config.load_kube_config()
>>> api_instance = ocpclient.CoreV1Api()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'CoreV1Api'
>>> api_instance = k8sclient.CoreV1Api()
>>> print(api_instance)
<kubernetes.client.apis.core_v1_api.CoreV1Api object at 0x7f9b2cb037d0>
```

Server SDK should handle pagination automatically. 
Code snippet:
```
with plivo.RestClient(auth_id = auth_id, auth_token = auth_token) as client:
    for e in client.endpoints:
        print e['_name']
```
Result is traceback:

```
TypeError                                 
Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-5-7acc734a5550> in <module>()
      1 with plivo.RestClient(auth_id = auth_id, auth_token = auth_token) as client:
----> 2     for e in client.endpoints:
      3         print e['_name']

~/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/plivo/base.pyc in gen()
    216             offset = 0
    217             while True:
--> 218                 response = self.list(limit=limit, offset=offset)
    219                 if not response.objects:
    220                     return

TypeError: list() got an unexpected keyword argument 'limit'

```

Hi there,

I noticed that in your [`clean()`](https://github.com/segmentio/analytics-python/blob/master/analytics/utils.py#L37-L48) function that sanitizes data before sending it to your API, if you encounter an unknown type, you will end up trying to coerce it to unicode, by using the [`_coerce_unicode()`](https://github.com/segmentio/analytics-python/blob/master/analytics/utils.py#L66-L76) function. Unfortunately, this doesn't play well with anything except strings (requires the `decode()` method). And sadly, you only log a warning [here](https://github.com/segmentio/analytics-python/blob/a74f9bd54596ecbce3c378236164ad19da473bb9/analytics/utils.py#L72).

We can do two things:
- really coerce the item to unicode by calling `unicode()` on it if it's not a string (and keep the call to `decode()` if and only if `isinstance(item, basestring)`
- enhance `logging` with more context so that when errors come up, we can actually retry sending the full event (and maybe log at error level)

We circumvented the errors in our codebase by checking that everything is JSON serializable before handing them to the SDK, but I really believe these are must-have enhancements :)

issue_title: Remove 'all' and 'any' models from to_mask API?
issue_body: The 'all' and 'any' modes are maybe not needed - one can just use 'exact' and check the result compared to 0 and 1. In fact, this is hard to implement internally because for 'all', the exact mode can sometimes give results are are extremely close but not exactly 1 - so then we have to introduce a tolerance, which gets messy. I'd actually vote to remove these modes and just keep 'center', 'subpixels', and 'exact'. What do others think?

issue_title: bootstrap-astropy.css messes up line numbering on sphinx code blocks
issue_body:
Currently working on the documentation for something I'm trying to get released as an astropy affiliated package, and I found a problem with how numbered code blocks are rendered using the astropy sphinx css stuff.

Here's a screenshot of what happens (at least with Chrome and Firefox) if I try to make a line-numbered code block in sphinx (using the code-block::-markup):
![sphinx_codeblock_wrong](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/401690/9363229/16c40582-469d-11e5-9116-a4457e23ea43.png)

The line numbering on that is not very useful, so it's a problem for my attempt at writing examples.

After a while of scrathing my head and learning how the element inspection thingy on Chrome works, I found out that this is because of something in bootstrap-astropy.css. Turns out that, for reasons I don't really understand, the line "white-space: pre-wrap;" in the pre-block (line 153 in bootstrap-astropy.css) is causing this problem. If I remove that line from the css, I get something a bit better-looking:
![sphinx_codeblock_lesswrong](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/401690/9363326/c3549d70-469d-11e5-8862-512ac2b74997.png)

Gonna modify the css in the thingy I'm working on to not cause the line numbering issue, but I figured you people would wanna know about this as well.

issue_title: BibCheck: migration to new code structure
issue_body:
- [ ] move to `invenio/modules/checker` (use `git mv`)
- [ ] use *Flask-Registry* instead of `PluginContainer`
- [x] **create SQLAlchemy model**

Originally on 2010-08-02

**Problem:**

```
In [17]: s.perform_request_search(p='Astrophysics')
Out[17]: [8, 11, 15]

In [18]: s.perform_request_search(p='Astrophysi*')
Out[18]: []
```

-*Cause:**

The global index is in Invenio stemmed by default and therefore the term stored for "Astrophysics" in the database is "Astrophys". When performing a wildcard search, the MySQL request is then "WHERE term LIKE 'astrophysi%'" and no results are found.

This problem is easily understandable for a developer who knows about the indexes and the stemming procedure however it is a surprising undesired "feature" for a normal user as the second search is the example is a broader search than the first one but returns less hits.
As `cookiecutter` is now based on `invenio-app` I think that `celery.py` and `wsgi.py` should be removed. In any case these two files are broken as they includes `factory.py` which has been removed.

issue_title: request: configuration option to enable multiple invenio instances to share an elastic search index
issue_body:
It is easy to configure multiple invenio instances to share the same PostgreSQL and Redis servers, by separating their data with different postgres roles / redis URLs. However, this is not currently possible with ElasticSearch as it lacks any sort of namespacing, and index names will collide if you point 2 invenio instances at the same elasticsearch instance. 

We are trying to work around this by manually specifying different index names for each instance, however this is fiddily.

Would it be possible to add an invenio-search configuration option that specifies a (unique) prefix to be used by a particular invenio instance? Or is there a better way to do this?

issue_body: Patched here: https://github.com/zopefoundation/ZEO/pull/15

First of all please see the discussion in #157.

Also, please consider issues and assumptions I tried to gather the following list:

1. Djoser assumes that the provided API should work even if `User` model is customised in many different ways which is still compatible with [`AbstractBaseUser`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/auth/customizing/#django.contrib.auth.models.AbstractBaseUser).
2. Because of point 1 there should be no direct usages of `User.USERNAME_FIELD`. It seems to be done correctly in the current code base.
3. Because of point 1 there should be no direct usages of `User.EMAIL_FIELD`. In the current code base there are a few of them, e.g. see changes in #157 and also see [here](https://github.com/sunscrapers/djoser/blob/e10d916/djoser/utils.py#L84) and [here](https://github.com/sunscrapers/djoser/blob/e10d916/djoser/utils.py#L96).
4. Djoser should provide some abstraction over `User.REQUIRED_FIELDS`. See #136 and #200.

On trying to build for 2.6.7 release, the Travis side is having issues:

https://github.com/MacPython/numexpr-wheels/

Some Linux builds are erroring and not passing, and some are still erroring but passing...

https://travis-ci.org/MacPython/numexpr-wheels/builds/414659538

Looks like it's some problem with older versions of NumPy (before 1.12.0b1) that permitted negative powers to be applied to integers. I was able to replicate it in a Virtualbox environment. The version check in `expressions.py` isn't working as intended:

    _np_version >= ('1', '12', '0b1')

It won't work for 1.9 or less, as the `'9'` is compared to the `'1'` in the minor version `'12'`.  I'm thinking we should drop the bug-release alphanumeric, and only compare on a 2-element tuple of `(major_ver, minor_ver)` cast to `int`s. The only way the bug-release version would matter if someone was working with an alpha of `numpy` 1.12, which seems unlikely.

As of the latest XML 1.0 Recommendation (Fifth Edition, 26 November 2008, referenced in namespace.py), the restrictions on name characters have been significantly relaxed, but RDFLib still enforces the restrictions outlined as of Fourth Edition (16 August 2006). 

In Fourth Edition, this URI is well-formed: http://some.uri/foo/C12345
But this one is not: http://some.uri/foo/12345

These are both valid URIs in Fifth Edition, which a number of free and commercial store providers support or (at least implicitly) require by virtue of implementation.

What are the implications to the project for supporting the loosened restrictions issued in Fifth Edition of the Recommendation, namely allowing start characters to be Unicode category "Nd"? Without that support, RDFLib will be incompatible with the kinds of triple store providers that implement patterns such as [base url]/[project]/[incremental ID] where [incremental ID] is numeric (Unicode category Nd).

Here is a relevant quote from the latest version of the Recommendation:
> This document is a W3C Recommendation. This fifth edition is not a new version of XML. As a convenience to readers, it incorporates the changes dictated by the accumulated errata (available at http://www.w3.org/XML/xml-V10-4e-errata) to the Fourth Edition of XML 1.0, dated 16 August 2006. In particular, erratum [E09] relaxes the restrictions on element and attribute names, thereby providing in XML 1.0 the major end user benefit currently achievable only by using XML 1.1. As a consequence, many possible documents which were not well-formed according to previous editions of this specification are now well-formed, and previously invalid documents using the newly-allowed name characters in, for example, ID attributes, are now valid.

issue_title: Can we get rid of wmult?
issue_body:
I'm working on #799 to reorganize how the draw commands work in order to provide the hooks for the sensor effects we want for LSST, and it occurs to me that the wmult parameter is a bit arcane, and is probably rarely (never?) used by end users.

I think it served a useful purpose when @gbernstein originally put it in, since we sometimes wanted to quickly tell GalSim to use a larger FFT size than the default.  But I think now that we have the gsparams stuff (in particular folding_threshold), that's really the more reliable way to adjust the behavior of stepk, and thus the FFT grid size.  wmult seems like a hack that we don't need anymore.

Any objections to my removing it in the reorg?

issue_title: Need better tests for plotting/viewing code

Once this is done, #592 should be rolled back.

issue_title: is_pid_running() needs to check if the process is actually postgres
issue_body:
Debugged a system where async executor was stuck in `_wait_for_postmaster_stop()`. There was a postmaster.pid left over from before power loss, the pid it contained was the same as the async executor threads PID, but different from Patroni pid. This means that the pid didn't match os.getpid(), but os.kill still succeeded.

I think we should also compare the start time in the pid file with the start time of the running process.

issue_title: Infrastructure and skeleton classes for aarch64 support
issue_body:
This is the tracking issue for the first step in aarch64 support. 

Task: Implement support for aarch64 binary paths as the argument to the Manticore constructor/linux classmethod.

Calling `Manticore(‘aarch64_elf’, [‘arg1’, ‘arg2’], dict(ENV=’env’))` and `Manticore.linux(‘aarch64_elf’, [‘arg1’, ‘arg2], dict(ENV=’env’))` should successfully execute, having loaded the binary and prepared the initial execution State. In this example, ‘aarch64_elf’ is the path to an aarch64 Linux ELF binary.

Completing this will involve modifying files including manticore.py, linux.py, cpufactory.py, and creating a new file in manticore/core/cpu for all the aarch64 code (aarch64.py? Not sure of the best name). The changes will involve modifying mostly the plumbing between components, modifying some logic, and creating new empty, skeleton classes for the Aarch64/Armv8 (naming?) cpu, Armv8 function abi, and Armv8 syscall abi. This task will not involve implementing an ELF loader, just connecting to one that already exists.

Changes that should also be implemented:
- Improve the error message Manticore gives for an unsupported binary arch. Currently KeyError, let’s raise an Exception with a good error message.
- Emit a logger warning that clearly states that Aarch64 support is experimental. This should be clearly visible to all users who pass an aarch64 binary to Manticore.

Please use the #manticore channel in the Empire Hacking slack to communicate with the Manticore development team while completing this task! https://empireslacking.herokuapp.com

issue_title: StreamResult wasSuccessful ignores unexpectedSuccess while TestResult method fails on any unexpectedSuccess
issue_body:
StreamResult method wasSuccessful() ignores the value of unexpectedSuccesses in determining whether the overall run was successful or not, however TestResult class does test the value of this attribute.

TestResult wasSuccessful() method result: https://github.com/testing-cabal/testtools/blob/master/testtools/testresult/real.py#L174
StreamResult wasSuccessful method result: https://github.com/testing-cabal/testtools/blob/master/testtools/testresult/real.py#L977

In testing changing StreamResult to align in behaviour with TestResult it appears that tests validating the behaviour against python2.7 fail.

While there are 3 tests that start failing once I changed the behaviour, the one (which is effectively called twice through inheritance) that suggest that it is intentional that StreamResult is defined at
https://github.com/testing-cabal/testtools/blob/master/testtools/tests/test_testresult.py#L223-L229
It results in the following two failures once StreamResult behaviour is changed to treat unexpectedSuccesses as failures:
`testtools.tests.test_testresult.TestAdaptedStreamResult.test_addUnexpectedSuccess_was_successful`
`testtools.tests.test_testresult.TestStreamToExtendedContract.test_addUnexpectedSuccess_was_successful`

Upstream python on changed this behaviour to determine the overall test results from unittest being successful sometime around 3.4 - https://bugs.python.org/issue20165

It's unclear if testtools is intentionally maintaining compatibility with unittest's TestResult class prior to this change in it's StreamResult class, or if this is just an oversight and StreamResult should be aligned with TestResult?

Currently this impacts stestr reporting of tests which I've logged as mtreinish/stestr#189 along with noting that it is possible to work around by changing to call the `TestResult.wasSuccessful()` method passing the StreamResult class instance in as the `self` argument.

I'm happy to submit a PR if someone can give some guidance on what should be the correct outcome above. Does StreamResult need to remain aligned with the python 2.7 contract, in which any fix needs to do some version detection and different depending on the python version, or is this an oversight in the enhancements that testtools is bringing to python unittests for 2.7?

issue_title: Creating a new datastore.entity with a key name is a pain
issue_body:
If I know I want to create a new Person named `jim`, it's a huge pain to get a handle on this entity.

What I would like to do:

```python
entity = dataset.entity(['Person', 'jim'])
entity['age'] = 80
entity.save()
```

What I currently have to do:

```python
key = Key.from_path(['Person', 'jim']).dataset(dataset)
entity = dataset.entity('Person').key(key)
entity['age'] = 80
entity.save()
```

This is a whole bunch of extra wiring I really shouldn't need to deal with.

issue_title: PubSub: add ack_id property accessor to subscriber Message class
issue_body:
Version: google-cloud-pubsub 0.35.4

This is a feature request to add a property accessor for ack_id in `google.cloud.pubsub_v1.subscriber.message.Message`.

Currently the `_ack_id` field is 'private', and only gets used via the `ack()` method (plus other related methods).

We find it useful to log the ack ID so that we can manually ack the ID using the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud` command line interface). This helps us get out of situations where the message is stuck in a (virtually) infinite retry loop because of some error.

We can just access the 'private' field, but it doesn't seem very nice to have to do that.

issue_title: StreamedResult.rows() is confusing and error prone
issue_body:
One of Cloud Spanner customers had a bug where they were iterating on StreamedResultSet.rows and were not getting any data back:
```
result = transaction.execute_sql(query)
        # here result.rows is empty
        for row in result.rows:
            print(row) # prints nothing
```

This is kind of working as intended because result.rows will only contain data when `consume_next` or `consume_all` is called. But it is still a very confusing API and easy to get wrong. It is also not clear to me at any point in time what rows will be present in this list. 
I think we should make the rows private and not expose it to the user. We should do it before marking the library as GA as this is a breaking change.

cc @lukesneeringer @tseaver @danoscarmike

issue_title: gcloud==0.18.3 and google-cloud-datastore==1.2.0 run into dependency conflicts
issue_body:
Hello,

I'm working on a large project with a lot of history that's using some Google Cloud services. We use [pip-compile](https://github.com/jazzband/pip-tools) for generating our requirements file. I installed google-cloud-datastore==1.2.0, which worked fine, but after adding it in my requirements file and running `pip-compile`, I'm running in a quagmire of dependency conflicts. I've looked at #3579, but that didn't enlighten me much.

This is the error that `pip-compile` generates:

```
$ pip-compile --verbose --output-file requirements.txt requirements.in | grep ' requires .* google-gax'
  gcloud==0.18.3            requires gax-google-logging-v2<0.9dev,>=0.8.0, gax-google-pubsub-v1<0.9dev,>=0.8.0, google-gax<0.13dev,>=0.12.3, googleapis-common-protos, grpc-google-logging-v2<0.9dev,>=0.8.0, grpc-google-pubsub-v1<0.9dev,>=0.8.0, grpcio>=1.0rc1, httplib2>=0.9.1, oauth2client>=2.0.1, protobuf!=3.0.0.b2.post1,>=3.0.0b2, six
  google-cloud-datastore==1.2.0 requires gapic-google-cloud-datastore-v1<0.16dev,>=0.15.0, google-cloud-core<0.27dev,>=0.26.0, google-gax<0.16dev,>=0.15.7
Could not find a version that matches google-gax<0.13dev,<0.16dev,>=0.12.3,>=0.15.7
```

As you can see, the problem is trying to determine the correct version of google-gax, which gcloud requires to be <0.13dev,>=0.12.3, while google-cloud-datastore requires to be <0.16dev,>=0.15.7. Obviously, this results in an error.

I'm not sure what the steps forward are here. I'm surprised that pip allowed installing and using google-cloud-datastore at all. I'm assuming that in itself broke the usage of gcloud, but that is used in a lot of different places in the code that I can't test now to verify that it broke.

e.g. http://jenkins.pantsbuild.org/job/pants_ci.integration_tests/21/SHARD=1/console and many others.

This became very apparent when we set up auto-scaling of jenkins workers--fresh workers fail ivy resolution _most_ of the time.  This is due to a combination of race conditions between global pants instances attempting to resolve, and ivy itself timing out when making requests to upstream repos.

We should probably considering holding a global file lock whenever we do ivy resolution in pants.  Most of the time the resolves are short because the artifacts are cached, so the lock doesn't have a high penalty.  But when the resolves are long, the probability of race errors is way too high.

If we split up resolution and fetching in ivy, that would make the penalty for holding the lock (I think it's only needed in resolution) even lower.

issue_title: Pants should support generation of targets for local go src
issue_body:
The `go list` tool provides everything needed to automatically generate go_library and go_binary targets for a local src tree when combined with pants resolve support for go.  A codegen task could either (re)create real BUILD files to fill in a full graph given a target root or else it could do so purely in-memory.  Having a such a task installed either as a top-level manual task (`./pants go-buildgen`) or else as installed as a `gen` task that implements [`alternate_target_roots`](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/core/tasks/task.py#L125-L133) and does complete dynamic generation transparently would ease transition for a go developer from their already very fast and hermetic low-overhead build system.  The only targets they'd need to define on disk in the latter case are those they wanted to address using pants on the command line (only the go_binary targets might be a common choice) and the remote library direct dependencies used by go code in the repo.

This idea grew out of a fruitful brainstorming session with @huckphin and @traviscrawford supplemented with feedback from @stuhood and @slyphon.

issue_title: Consider switching to "new style" cffi callbacks
issue_body: To avoid needing a compile step, we currently use "old style" cffi callbacks. The new style are purportedly faster and less error prone, but they require an additional build step, and so we would want to confirm the speedup before switching.

issue_title: Export synthetic jar path for external junit run
issue_body:
Currently IntelliJ junit runner will load up classfiles for all targets imported in a project. For example, when `x::` is imported to intellij, and user invokes a test that only concerns `x/y/z:foo`, classfiles for all targets in x:: will be loaded. 

Since pants creates a synthetic jar before its own junit run, also having tested intellij junit by feeding the synthetic jar manually, I propose that pants exports the synthetic for intellij, which will have the following benefits:
1. intellij junit runner will be as hygienic as pants junit run.
2. it simplifies the existing interface of classfile collection by target ids.

Question is where to call this function. I am thinking adding a flag `--create-synthetic-jar` under `export-classpath` goal. Feedback welcome.

issue_title: Implement CLI > target > config logic in the options system.
issue_body:
For some targets (like jvm_app) we want to have options at target level, in addition to options at CLI and config file level.

The precedence will be CLI > target > config.

Currently this logic is in pants/backend/jvm/tasks/bundle_create.py, but if we see more use cases for target level options, this logic should go to options system.

issue_title: Insert MathML as plain text for Word 2010 to render it as an equation
issue_body:
Hi everybody,

I am trying to insert some MathML code into a *.docx document. To do so, I do the following:

```
#!/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf8 -*-

from docx import Document


doc = Document('template.docx')

# Word needs a specific XML namespace in order to convert MathML into a graphic math area
# The MathML expression must contain no newline
math_xml = '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>'

# Insert the MathML into Word
doc.add_paragraph().add_run(text=math_xml, style=None).font.math = True
doc.save('test.docx')
```

When I open the resulting document in Word 2010, [this is what I see](http://image.noelshack.com/fichiers/2016/38/1474546928-word.jpg).
The math area has been correctly created, but the code itself has not been rendered.

The interesting thing is when I open the resulting document and then [copy](http://image.noelshack.com/fichiers/2016/38/1474547629-word.jpg) then [paste](http://image.noelshack.com/fichiers/2016/38/1474547168-word.jpg) the MathML code from/to the math area using "Paste as plain text" directly from Word UI, it gets [properly rendered](http://image.noelshack.com/fichiers/2016/38/1474547408-word.jpg).

I have come to the conclusion that when a style is applied to the MathML text, then Word does not render it as an equation. And this assertion is verified by the fact that when you use another paste option like "Keep source formatting" or "Melt both formats", then the equation is not rendered using Word UI.

My question is: **how can I render the text as plain text without any style using python-docx lib ?**
I tried those, but none of them worked:

```
doc.add_paragraph().add_run(text=math_xml, style=None).font.math = True
doc.add_paragraph().add_run(text=math_xml, style='').font.math = True
doc.add_paragraph().add_run(text=math_xml).font.math = True
```

Or maybe this is currently not supported by this library ?

Thanks for your help :)

issue_title: Multiple forms: no check which one was actually submitted
issue_body:
Though there is `form_plugin_id` submitted with each form, there is no check if we are treating the right form when processing them - this is a problem, when there are forms. For example, validation is always done on every form, no matter if it was submitted. This is a bad thing, as in my case, can lead to some lost hours, because a recaptcha did not validate, when not in the first form...ha (aldryn_form validates all of them, so only the first is valid...).

As my project (that I took over...) already had a custom aldryn_forms, I quickly patched the `FormPlugin` (in cms_plugins.py) with this line, at several places:

    if request.POST.get('form_plugin_id') == str(instance.id):

Dont know if this an acceptable solution.

In the worst case, this bug will lead to a wrong form to be submitted (in my case, all but recaptcha was optional - the first form was processed, though I clicked submit in the second one...what lead to even more confusion, I thought my recaptcha is working ;).

Following the manual install steps doesn't work
Readme references 'bin' directory that doesn't exist in repository

Readme notes to check out charm-tools from bzr instead of github

The install, upgrade-charm, start, stop etc. hooks in the reactive basic layer all use '#!/bin/env python' as the shebang line, which is Python2. Rather than force a version of Python that has been deprecated for several years and will soon stop being on the cloud images by default, 'charm build' should default to generating Python3 stubs and perhaps provide an option to use Python2 when compatibility is needed with legacy code.

layer.yaml could be used to declare if Python2 compatibility is needed.

If both Python2 and Python3 are supported, 'charm build' will need to detect and correct shebang lines somehow. The edge case here being a Python3 charm that includes copies of legacy Python2 scripts (eg. administrative scripts to be invoked from cron).

issue_title: Serial DFU pinging fails if bootloader is not ready
issue_body:
Serial DFU could fail if bootloader board is not ready when trying to ping. This could happen during a combined DFU.

In dfu_transport_serial.py in open() the bootloader board is pinged twice.
If the first __ping() fails the complete DFU operation fails. It looks like the first __ping() should be removed.

Also, when looking into the __ping() function, I see that instead of returning an error code, it will raise an exception when the bootloader board is not responding. This will break the "waiting for ping"-functionality in open().

Users should not have to worry about basic types serialization such as ```Datetime``` when passing body and querystring arguments to requests.
Currently, such formats break when are passed to ``json.dumps``.

issue_title: OSError: 'File name too long' when caching AWS S3 files
issue_body:
The [allennlp-as-a-library-example](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp-as-a-library-example) downloads its example data and models from an AWS S3 server and caches them on the filesystem, but I'm getting the following error:

```
0it [00:00, ?it/s]2018-05-15 16:04:48,626 - INFO - allennlp.common.file_utils - https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/allennlp/datasets/academic-papers-example/train.jsonl not found in cache, downloading to /tmp/tmphvj02xzn
                                                               2018-05-15 16:04:55,187 - INFO - allennlp.common.file_utils - copying /tmp/tmphvj02xzn to cache at /home/goodmami/.allennlp/datasets/aHR0cHM6Ly9zMy11cy13ZXN0LTIuYW1hem9uYXdzLmNvbS9hbGxlbm5scC9kYXRhc2V0cy9hY2FkZW1pYy1wYXBlcnMtZXhhbXBsZS90cmFpbi5qc29ubA==.dfeac5182442931a79450b22cef3816b-7
Traceback (most recent call last):
  [...omitted...]
  File "/home/goodmami/env/allennlp/lib/python3.6/site-packages/allennlp/common/file_utils.py", line 125, in get_from_cache
    shutil.copyfile(temp_filename, cache_path)
  File "/home/goodmami/env/allennlp/lib/python3.6/shutil.py", line 121, in copyfile
    with open(dst, 'wb') as fdst:
OSError: [Errno 36] File name too long: '/home/goodmami/.allennlp/datasets/aHR0cHM6Ly9zMy11cy13ZXN0LTIuYW1hem9uYXdzLmNvbS9hbGxlbm5scC9kYXRhc2V0cy9hY2FkZW1pYy1wYXBlcnMtZXhhbXBsZS90cmFpbi5qc29ubA==.dfeac5182442931a79450b22cef3816b-7'
```

I'm using AllenNLP 0.4.2, installed via Pip, on Pop!_OS 17.10 (an Ubuntu derivative).

The method of caching files using a base64-encoded version of their remote URL (see [allennlp.common.file_utils](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/b71ef434b310e9b1ba400b42c7334c794996b40e/allennlp/common/file_utils.py))  is clever but not scalable as URL length limits (usually ~2000 characters) are much larger than file name lengths (usually no more than 255 characters (see [here](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparison_of_file_systems#Limits)). Generally this issue won't come up, as a URL such as `https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/allennlp/datasets/academic-papers-example/train.jsonl` (88 characters) is 155 characters when base64-encoded. However, if a user encrypts their home directory with [ecryptfs](http://ecryptfs.org/), the maximum file name length is reduced to 143 characters (see [here](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ecryptfs/+bug/344878) and [here](https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/32834/258365)), which leads to the problem shown above. Encryptfs is a file-based encryption system, and I don't think that full-disk encryption (e.g., with LUKS) suffers the same limitation, but encryptfs is a widely used implementation and is enabled by default on some Linux distributions. If it wasn't clear above, note that the issue *can* affect users who don't use encryptfs, but it requires URLs about 2--3x longer to hit the 255-character limit.

Some possible solutions:
* Break up the URL and base64-encoding each part such that each non-final part is a directory (e.g., `b64encode("https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com")` becomes a directory containing `b64encode("/allennlp/datasets/academic-papers-example/train.jsonl")`). This solution only mitigates the issue a bit, but doesn't solve it.
* Store the files in a non-encrypted part of the filesystem, e.g., `/opt/allennlp-data/...`, but this is a terrible solution: it introduces permissions issues and doesn't change the 255 character limit, and the user may actually want the data encrypted.
* Use an index to map URLs to some new filename (e.g., using Python's `tempfile` module)

I think the last solution is best. Since you already have a directory for storing the cached files, it should be painless to add a `index.json` file or something.

issue_title: from_DSN won't work with upper case passwords
issue_body:
Hello,

If the password given in the dsn string to InfluxDBClient.from_DSN contains some upper case letters, then subsequent queries on the client will fail with:
```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/influxdb/influxdb08/client.py", line 419, in query
    chunked=chunked)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/influxdb/influxdb08/client.py", line 447, in _query
    expected_response_code=200
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/influxdb/influxdb08/client.py", line 255, in request
    raise InfluxDBClientError(response.content, response.status_code)
influxdb.influxdb08.client.InfluxDBClientError: 401: Invalid username/password
```

Good practice for passwords is to mix lower and upper cases.
I guess other params like username and database name in the dsn could also have this problem if they are case sensitive as well. I did not test them.

For the moment it can be solved by giving the password as an extra parameter to from_DSN. But it could confuse other users and take them a while to understand.


Cheers,
Axel

issue_title: Only load the registry if it's needed
issue_body:
For example, the registry shouldn't be loaded when a `DataPackage` is created as `datapackage.DataPackage('/path/to/datapackage.json', '/path/to/schema.json')`. The schema path is being passed explicitly, to the registry isn't used at all.

To do this, we would have to change both `Schema._load_schema` and `Schema._load_validator`. The biggest complicator is deciding if a string contains a file, a URL, or a schema ID, and load the registry or not, depending on the answer. An easier solution can be simply defaulting to an empty registry if the actual one couldn't be loaded, but then we have to make sure we at least log the exception to avoid creating some hard-to-find bugs

The reason for fixing this issue is to allow using this library even if there're problems loading the registry (e.g. the local cache file is inacessible for some reason like https://github.com/frictionlessdata/tabulator-py/issues/48).

issue_title: Slow check of DSC step signature
issue_body:
It has been observed that for [this regression benchmark](https://github.com/stephenslab/dsc-reg/tree/4ca90b28c77d0c26084def33578bd62b941f70a9), it takes ~500 sec to run `--replicate 20`, but to rerun without any code change it takes ~70 sec. Notice that ~70 sec is also what `--replicate 1` takes, so this rules out the slowness checking on file signatures. 

That brings back to another SoS design that has led to this bottleneck. Long story short is that this design was added a while ago to exclusively address to DSC's typical usage of running large amount of small jobs on a single machine. But there was certain implementation hurdles due to Python's GIL (compared to a typical SoS task that does not run inside Python child processes) so we always have trouble limiting maximum running processeses. This is a known Python multiprocessing module problem. We ended up having to use file lock for each CPU thread. That is why at the beginning of each module block (can contain many module instances) there will be a brief halt for lock releases. But the delay is mostly invariant to the number of instances in the module (`--replicate`).

We can try improving SoS engineering. In the mean time, here is a temp solution:

1. Have a fresh run of `dsc file.dsc --replicate 20`
2. For the specific module you have added, just run `dsc file.dsc --target "simulate * varbvs * score" --replicate 20`. This will be faster but will rebuild the query database to a smaller one.
3. After you are done with everything, run `dsc file.dsc --replicate --skip all`. With `--skip all` it will skip all the running part, but just build the query database.

Have not tested it a lot, but this is designed to work at least ... Maybe give it a shot?

issue_title: New nodes should be members, not proxies, on the etcd cluster
issue_body:
When you have a running deis cluster and AWS autoscaling replaces 1 node, etcd will come up as a proxy and not a member on the etcd cluster.

You can promote a proxy to a member but you have to manually do it https://github.com/coreos/etcd/blob/master/Documentation/proxy.md#promote-a-proxy-to-a-member-of-etcd-cluster.

You can also add the node as a member when it boots https://github.com/coreos/etcd/blob/master/Documentation/runtime-configuration.md#add-a-new-member but you have to set ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER and ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER_STATE instead of using a discovery url.

issue_title: conda does not fail correctly when out of disk
issue_body: I recently tried to install a package.  When I ran the command, I ran out of disk and the installation failed.  However, instead of completely cleaning the package installation, conda left a broken package installation in my `pkgs` directory.  This was confusing and potentially dangerous.

Spawn a container for centos 6:
```
sudo docker run --rm --privileged -v $PWD/root:/root -it centos:6 /bin/bash
```

Run the following commands:
```
curl -LO https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda2-4.3.31-Linux-x86_64.sh
bash Miniconda2-4.3.31-Linux-x86_64.sh -bp m2
m2/bin/conda update conda -y
m2/bin/conda install conda-build -y
mkdir -p /channel/{linux-64,noarch}
cp -rv m2/pkgs/*.tar.bz2 /channel/linux-64/
m2/bin/conda index /channel/noarch/
m2/bin/conda index /channel/linux-64
cat <<EOF > ~/.condarc
channels:
  - file:///channel/linux-64
EOF
m2/bin/conda update conda
```

issue_title: conda install fails after cloning the root env (conda-env leaks into the new env)
issue_body:
Two in a day! :)

On a fresh install of Linux x64 Anaconda, I cloned the root env like so:

`$ ~/anaconda2/bin/conda create --name sk1 --clone root`

Then I went to install a package, and I saw:

```
$ conda install -c conda-forge pylibmc==1.5.1
Fetching package metadata .........
Solving package specifications: ..........


InstallError: Install error: Error: one or more of the packages already installed depend on 'conda'
and should only be installed in the root environment: conda-env
These packages need to be removed before conda can proceed.
```

Maybe there should be a special case to not clone over `conda-env`?

I've installed `atlas` package into the `_build` environment as a dependency for a library that I try to build with `conda`.

Now I have the following issue: library `<conda-build-env>/lib/liblapack.a` says that it has undefined symbol `_gfortran_copy_string`. If I install `system` package, then I have `libgfortran.so.1.0.0` that contains the above symbol.

However, `atlas` package doesn't have `system` package as a dependency.

It seems to me that `atlas` must have `system` package as a dependency.

UPDATE: actually, the problem is even deeper. `Atlas` installs only static libraries (*.a). Therefore, it doesn't have any references to libgfortran.so.1.0.0, although it definitely depends on it.

issue_title: conda sends bad HTTP headers when querying noarch repos
issue_body:
``conda 4.3`` requires package repositories to provide the ``noarch`` platform directory. After adding ``noarch`` to my custom repository served by Microsoft ``IIS 7.5``, conda started failing after a few runs of ``conda install``:

```
$ conda install conda
Fetching package metadata ....
CondaHTTPError: HTTP 400 BAD REQUEST for url <http://myrepo/packages/noarch/repodata.json>
An HTTP error occurred when trying to retrieve this URL.
HTTPError(u'400 Client Error: Bad Request for url: http://myrepo/packages/noarch/repodata.json',)
```

Inspecting the HTTP headers sent by conda, it seems that the ETag ``If-None-Match`` contains bad characters:

```
$ conda install conda --debug
DEBUG conda.core.index:fetch_repodata(368): Locally invalidating cached repodata for http://myrepo/packages/noarch at C:\Anaconda2\pkgs\cache\8beb19b1.json
DEBUG requests.packages.urllib3.connectionpool:_make_request(400): http://dproxy:8080 "GET http://myrepo/packages/noarch/repodata.json HTTP/1.1" 400 311
DEBUG conda.core.index:fetch_repodata_remote_request(193):
>>GET /packages/noarch/repodata.json HTTP
> User-Agent: conda/4.3.4 requests/2.12.4 CPython/2.7.13 Windows/7 Windows/6.1.7601
> Accept: */*
> Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, compress, identity
> Connection: keep-alive
> Content-Type: application/json
> If-Modified-Since: Mon, 16 Jan 2017 15:27:55 GMT
> If-None-Match: \"b7eece1bd70d21:0\"

<<HTTP 400 Bad Request
< Content-Type: text/html; charset=us-ascii
< Date: Mon, 16 Jan 2017 15:30:47 GMT
< Proxy-Connection: Keep-Alive
< Server: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0
< Content-Length: 311
< Elapsed: 00:00.006000

```
As you can see, the value of ``If-None-Match`` contains extra ``\"``, which IIS interprets as invalid. Stripping out these characters makes the request go through the server without any problems. 

Note that this happens only when conda queries the ``noarch`` subdirectory. All other platform subdirs work fine. Also note that this does not happen immediately; it takes a few retries until conda starts sending the malformed header.

I'm facing a problem with the update_dependencies option.

On the one hand, when running "conda install ..." I've noticed that conda tries to upgrade packages that I didn't ask it to upgrade (explicitly or implicitly). This is problematic because I don't necessarily want every installed package upgraded to the latest version every time I run "install", because my code may not be compatible w/ that version yet. The only solution for this that I know of is to set **update_dependencies = False**.

On the other hand, suppose I have version 2 of package B installed, and then I run "conda install A==10" where version 10 of package A depends on version **3** of package B. I would expect that B is at that point upgraded, and it is upgraded as long as **update_dependencies = True**.

So, it seems like I have to choose between one desired behavior or the other. 

Ideally, conda would first crawl the dependency tree for what I'm asking it to install. If a package lies outside of that tree, conda would try to keep it on the same version. If a package lies inside of that tree, conda would either keep that package on the same version (update_dependencies = False) or remove the current version from the spec (update_dependencies = True). I think the problem with the existing code is that it incorrectly assumes all installed packages to be in the dependency tree.

Here's the relevant section of code https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/resolve.py#L682

It'd be nice to have a `conda install -dependencies-only` flag. This would install all of the dependencies of the required package, but not the package itself. This would be useful when developing a package - it would allow you to install all of the required packages in one fell swoop.

issue_title: pandas 0.16 can't be imported from the root environment on windows
issue_body:
Pandas 0.15.* works fine in the root environment on windows:

```shell
C:\Users\kalev>conda update python
python                    2.7.6                         0

C:\Users\kalev>python -c "import pandas"
```

After upgrading to 0.16, pandas is not longer importable:

```shell
C:\Users\kalev>conda update pandas
The following packages will be UPDATED:

C:\Users\kalev>python -c "import pandas"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "C:\Anaconda\lib\site-packages\pandas\__init__.py", line 47, in <module>
    import pandas.core.config_init
  File "C:\Anaconda\lib\site-packages\pandas\core\config_init.py", line 17, in <module>
    from pandas.core.format import detect_console_encoding
  File "C:\Anaconda\lib\site-packages\pandas\core\format.py", line 24, in <module>
    from pandas.tseries.period import PeriodIndex, DatetimeIndex
  File "C:\Anaconda\lib\site-packages\pandas\tseries\period.py", line 8, in <module>
    import pandas.tseries.frequencies as frequencies
  File "C:\Anaconda\lib\site-packages\pandas\tseries\frequencies.py", line 9, in <module>

It works fine in a virtual environment, where ``python`` can be updated to 2.7.9. But 2.7.6 and 2.7.8 appear to be incompatible with ``pandas 0.16``. Is there a way to update the python package in the root environment?

issue_title: conda info to show a bit more about operating system
issue_body:
There is some general architecture and bit-width information in the `conda info` output but it would be really handy for debugging if we provided a few more hints that would allow someone looking at the output to make a good guess as to:

* Windows version (7, 8, 10, etc)
* Linux variant and version (CentOS, RHEL, SUSE, etc.)
* Special privileges of user running `conda` command (root, Administrator)
* Variations in ownership between "root" Conda environment and user executing `conda info`

issue_title: [Feature request] To download remote files into local without keeping the same virtual path of remote files but only file names
issue_body:
REQUEST:  to be able to download remote files into local without keeping the same virtual path of remote files but only file names

Here is a simple example:
```
<MyBlobStorage>: <MyContainer>/<SubDirectory1>/<SubDirectory2>/TargetFile.txt
<MyLocalVM>:/tmp
```

Suppose I want to download TargetFile.txt in blob storage to /tmp in my local VM, I run blobxfer by specifying remote path and local path with --remote-path and --local-path respectively like this:
```
blobxfer download --remote-path <MyContainer>/<SubDirectory1>/<SubDirectory2> --local-path /tmp ...
```
Then, blobxfer will attempt to download TargetFile.txt in blob storage to local VM with including path, keeping the same virtual file path like this:
```
<MyLocalVM>:/tmp/<SubDirectory1>/<SubDirectory2>/TargetFile.txt
```
But what I would like to achieve is simply to download files to local directory without keeping virtual path (virtual sub directory) like this:
```
<MyLocalVM>:/tmp/TargetFile.txt
```

Giving another example, 
Suppose I have the following 3 files in my Azure blob storage,
```
<MyBlobStorage>: <MyContainer>/<SubDirectory1>/<SubDirectory2>/TargetFile1.txt
<MyBlobStorage>: <MyContainer>/<SubDirectory1>/TargetFile2.txt
<MyBlobStorage>: <MyContainer>/TargetFile3.txt
```
I try to download the files above to my local VM /tmp directory by running the following command:
```
blobxfer download --remote-path <MyContainer> --local-path /tmp 
```
I would like to download files to local directory like this:
```
<MyLocalVM>$ ls -1  /tmp
TargetFile1.txt
TargetFile2.txt
TargetFile3.txt
```

issue_title: az container create with yaml file and secureValue fails with not supported before version 2018-06-01
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
When I try to use the az container create with a yaml file it fails when new features like secureValue for an environment variable or connecting to Log Analytics with the message:

az : ERROR: The secure environment variable is not supported before version '2018-06-01'. 
At line:1 char:1 

**To Reproduce**
Create the following yaml file as secure-env.yaml (taken from the docs: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-instances/container-instances-environment-variables):
```
apiVersion: 2018-06-01
location: westus
name: securetest
properties:
  containers:
  - name: mycontainer
    properties:
      environmentVariables:
        - "name": "SECRET"
          "secureValue": "my-secret-value"
        - "name": "NOTSECRET"
          "value": "my-exposed-value"
      image: nginx
      ports: []
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 1.0
          memoryInGB: 1.5
  osType: Linux
  restartPolicy: Always
tags: null
type: Microsoft.ContainerInstance/containerGroups
```
Create the resource group
```
az group create --location westus --name myRG
```
Attempt to create the container with the yaml file:
```
az container create --resource-group myRG --name securetest -f secure-env.yaml
```

**Expected behavior**
The container should be created using the secureValue provided in the yaml file.

**Environment summary**
I'm running Window 10, CLI version 2.0.38 and conatainer (0.1.24)

**Additional context**
The sample code on this page fails too with the same error when trying to connect to Azure Log Analytics: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-instances/container-instances-log-analytics

The Cloud Shell does seem to work in the Azure Portal but the latest CLI running on my machine does not.

### Description
Thanks @DheerendraRathor for helping with the debugging of the issue.

Our service is returning the string 'PT34.8179112S' for duration. This is getting deserialized into the duration object correctly. Then we are trying to return this object back to IO stream where the duration object seems to be serialized to the string '0:00:34.817911'. Then we are passing this serialized string to the next command. Here we try to deserialize this string again and the code fails in isoduration.py in parsing. Essentially, we're not able to "deserialize - serialize - deserialize" the duration object. 

This is an investigation by my colleague @DheerendraRathor about the issue:
1.	Autorest deserializes duration to python timedelta (It expects ISO 8601 due to Microsoft standards)
https://github.com/Azure/msrest-for-python/blob/master/msrest/serialization.py#L1062 
    def deserialize_duration(attr):
        """Deserialize ISO-8601 formatted string into TimeDelta object.
        :param str attr: response string to be deserialized.
        :rtype: TimeDelta
        :raises: DeserializationError if string format invalid.
        """
        try:
            duration = isodate.parse_duration(attr)
        except(ValueError, OverflowError, AttributeError) as err:
            msg = "Cannot deserialize duration object."
            raise_with_traceback(DeserializationError, msg, err)
        else:
            return duration
 
                isodate.parse_duration returns python datetime.timedelta
 
2.	Now as Anudeep pointed out timedelta is directly converted to str during todict conversion: https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/master/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/util.py#L166 
During this conversion ISO 8601 format of duration is lost as Python str conversion of timedelta is “x days, h:mm:ss”
>>> str(timedelta(days=2, weeks=1, hours=2))
'9 days, 2:00:00'
>>> str(timedelta(hours=1, minutes=10))
'1:10:00'
3.	Now as Anudeep is trying to deserialize it again, it will fail as python str(timedelta) is not ISO 8601 and msrest deserializers expects ISO 8601 format.
 
Possible solution:
-	Use isodate.duration_isoformat method to retain ISO 8601 format for duration while creating dictionary. 
-	Leverage msrest Serializer for dictionary conversion https://github.com/Azure/msrest-for-python/blob/master/msrest/serialization.py#L614 
Which is internally using isodate.duration_isoformat
---

### Environment summary

issue_title: NoneType error: `az storage entity query --table-name mytable`
issue_body:
# Describe the bug
Command `az storage entity query --table-name "mytable"` returns:
```
az storage entity query: error: 'NoneType' object is not iterable
```

This appears to be affecting versions:
- `azure-cli=2.0.32-1~wheezy`
- `azure-cli=2.0.31-1~wheezy`

32-1 appears to have been removed, but 31-1 is still serving from repo. I was not able to successfully get the command run until downgraded to 30-1.

## Workaround
We install Azure CLI to VSTS Hosted Linux Agent(s) - so "clean" environments. We have to regress two versions:

```
apt-get update -y
apt-get install -y python libssl-dev libffi-dev python-dev build-essential jq tree
curl -L https://azurecliprod.blob.core.windows.net/install.py -o install.py
printf "/usr/azure-cli\n/usr/bin" | python install.py

apt-get update -y
apt-get install -y azure-cli=2.0.30-1~wheezy --allow-downgrades
```

# To Reproduce
1. Install latest azure-cli:
```
apt-get update -y
apt-get install -y python libssl-dev libffi-dev python-dev build-essential jq tree
curl -L https://azurecliprod.blob.core.windows.net/install.py -o install.py
printf "/usr/azure-cli\n/usr/bin" | python install.py
```
2. Export AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING for valid table storage account/table.
2. Run command: `az storage entity query --table-name "mytable"`
2. Observe error.

## Expected behavior
Return the contents of the table

# Environment summary
Env: Ubuntu 16.04.4 LTS
Env2: VSTS hosted Linux Agent

Install Method:
```
apt-get update -y
apt-get install -y python libssl-dev libffi-dev python-dev build-essential jq tree
curl -L https://azurecliprod.blob.core.windows.net/install.py -o install.py
printf "/usr/azure-cli\n/usr/bin" | python install.py
```

print "DONE"

Best Regards

issue_title: Question: Using hooks at different stages
issue_body:
1. In order to utilize hooks that run in different stages, pre-commit needs to install itself as the hook in each one (e.g. "pre-commit" and "commit-msg"). By default however, pre-commit only installs itself in "pre-commit" and you can't specify multiple types with the -t argument. It would be nice if pre-commit would see that a hook in the config can only be used in a specific stage (e.g. "commit-msg") and install itself there rather than requiring explicit knowledge and direction from the user.
1. Most hooks that don't explicitly limit themselves to a single stage, so if pre-commit is installed as hook multiple times (e.g. "pre-commit" and "commit-msg"), you'll get multiple runs of the unrestricted hooks for each commit. To avoid that, in your config file, you'd be required to specify a single stage for each hook. This can become pretty redundant.

For example:
```
repos:
- repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
  rev: v1.3.0
  hooks:
  # Git state
  - id: check-merge-conflict
    stages: [commit]
  - id: check-added-large-files
    stages: [commit]
  # Sensitive information
  - id: detect-private-key
    stages: [commit]
  - id: detect-aws-credentials
    stages: [commit]
  # Generic file state
  - id: trailing-whitespace
    stages: [commit]
  - id: mixed-line-ending
    stages: [commit]
  - id: end-of-file-fixer
    stages: [commit]
  - id: check-executables-have-shebangs
    stages: [commit]
  # Language syntax/formatting
  - id: check-yaml
    stages: [commit]
  - id: check-json
    stages: [commit]
  - id: pretty-format-json
    stages: [commit]
- repo: https://github.com/mattlqx/pre-commit-sign
  rev: v1.1.1
  hooks:
  - id: sign-commit
```

Am I understanding the limitations of stages correctly? Can anything be improved from the framework side to make commit and commit-msg stages not duplicate work or be more intelligent about what runs where? Maybe keep track of if a hook ran in the commit stage and avoid running it in the commit-msg stage?

Is there a way to add a hook to `.pre-commit-config.yaml` but have it always skip unless it's called explicitly? Granted, this is a weird use-case where I'm basically just trying to benefit from `pre-commit`'s package management abilities :)
So for example, while doing `git commit` or `pre-commit run` it would be marked as `Skipped` but would run when called like this:
```
pre-commit run <hook>
```

For context, I'm trying to simplify setting up [prmd](https://github.com/interagent/prmd) (a Ruby package) on Python repos. This already works (as part of a script)
`pre-commit run --verbose prmd --files <json>`
where
```
-   repo: /path/to/mirrors-prmd
    sha: v0.11.7
    hooks:
    -   id: prmd
        entry: prmd render
        args:
        - --template=main/api/doc/templates
```
but now I would like to prevent this hook from running in all other instances :)
As I said, weird use-case... maybe I just need to go to sleep now 😴

issue_title: feature: check for .pre-commit-hooks.yaml when parsing local repo
issue_body: When parsing `repo: local` in .pre-commit-config.yaml, pre-commit should check to see if the information exists in a local .pre-commit-hooks.yaml. Currently it does not, which can lead to duplication of the hook definition when making new pre-commit hooks repos.

issue_title: detect_aws_credentials hook raises a false positive match when AWS credential values contain only a space character(s)
issue_body:
The detect_aws_credentials hook raises a false positive match when AWS credential values contain only a space character(s). For example, if the ~/.aws/credentials file contains the following:

```
[test]
aws_access_key_id = ggfgafg
aws_secret_access_key = 
```
By having a space character after the "=" for the _aws_secret_access_key_ variable it results in:
```
Detect AWS Credentials...................................................Failed
hookid: detect-aws-credentials

AWS secret found in test.txt: ****************************
```

Which is technically true, but in essence the hook should trim (remove) the space(s) in the values for the variables prior to checking the file(s) to prevent this confusing issue from occurring.

Is it correct behavior for method `Record.annotate`?

```
def annotate(self, value, ts):
        if value == CLIENT_SEND:
            self.kind(CLIENT)
            self._timestamp = ts
        elif value == SERVER_RECEIVED:
            self.kind(SERVER)
            self._timestamp = ts
        elif value == CLIENT_RECEIVED:
            self.kind(CLIENT)
            self.finish(ts)
        elif value == SERVER_SEND:
            self.kind(SERVER)
            self.finish(ts)
        else:
            v = {'value': value, 'timestamp': int(ts)}
            self._annotations.append(v)
        return self
```

What if user want to send `"cs"` value as annotation, like `span.annotate('cs')`? And user want to see it as annotation, not as timestamp correction.
For example, I log every request body of inbound requests in my API and some one sent `sr` in request body. In this case, I will not see anything in the annotation.
It can be any other example in which we store some random data as an annotation.

If you want to keep the current behavior, you can make the value of `CLIENT_SEND` (and others) as instance of the class inherited from `str`. And in the `annotate` method check for `isinstance`, not for equivalence
We need to add documentation, as a first step, just create simple stub and add README.rst information and probably examples.

issue_title: Use Linux distro CA certs when available
issue_body:
Most Linux distros already provide a trusted set of CA certificates with tools to adjust the certificates as needed. It would be really nice if certifi (i.e. requests) used these rather than the shipped CA bundle. As it turns out, at least Fedora, Debian and SUSE patch their certifi to use the system CA bundle:

https://src.fedoraproject.org/rpms/python-certifi/blob/master/f/certifi-2016.9.26-remove-bundle-cert.patch
https://salsa.debian.org/debian/python-certifi/blob/debian/master/debian/patches/0001-Use-Debian-provided-etc-ssl-certs-ca-certificates.cr.patch
https://build.opensuse.org/package/view_file/devel:languages:python/python-certifi/python-certifi-shipped-requests-cabundle.patch?expand=1

That's all well and good until pip pulls in a new version of certifi and now you're back to the bundled CA certificates.

Would you accept a patch that tries the above paths on Linux before falling back to the bundled CA certs?

issue_title: notebook_loaded.Notebook fires before extensions are loaded?
Prevent re-execution of a cell that is already running
issue_body:
If you start the second cell below and then run it multiple times (e.g. with shift+enter) while it is already running, the value of i will be equal to the number of times it was run.

```
# initialization cell
i = 0
```

```
# cell to run concurrently
import time
time.sleep(5)
print("done %d"  % i)
i = i + 1
```

We ran into this in the context of long-running spark jobs, where a user mistakenly ran an already-running (long-lived) cell, leading to errors. 

It seems like an easier-to-understand behavior would be simply to ignore "run cell" commands for a cell that is already running.

issue_title: /files/ should use StaticFileHandler when files are local
issue_body:
Right now, the ContentsManager is used for requests on `/files/`. This is the most generic and will work for any ContentsManager, but is very inefficient when it's not needed. For the default case of FileContentsManager, we should go back to using AuthenticatedFileHandler, which is now unused (#865).

The simplest way to express this, I think, is a files_handler_class attribute on the ContentsManager. The base class should set the default to what we use now, and FileContentsManager should set it to AuthenticatedFileHandler.

issue_title: The sigmoid definition in the tutorial is wrong
issue_body:
Hi there, 

Just a small point.

Reading through the [tutorial](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/master/docs/tutorial.md), the definition of sigmoid is given in terms of tanh as

```
def sigmoid(x):
    return 0.5*(np.tanh(x) + 1)
```

But should it not be

```
def sigmoid(x):
    return 0.5*(np.tanh(x/2) + 1)
```

For example sigmoid(1) with the first method gives ~0.88, with the second method gives the expected ~0.73.

This obviously isn't a huge issue but could be misleading if people just used the example code without double checking!

issue_title: Config to allow decorators that function like @classmethod
issue_body:
In SQLAlchemy, there is a `declared_attr` decorator that creates class methods:
http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/orm/extensions/declarative/api.html#sqlalchemy.ext.declarative.declared_attr

As a result, my codebase is sprinkled with:
```
class SomeTable(db.Model):
    @declared_attr
    def __table_args__(cls):  # noqa: N805
        ...
```

It would be nice if we could have a config option that lets us declare if a decorator results in a `staticmethod` or `classmethod` as opposed to the implicit default of it being an instance method. I was thinking something like using comma separated list in the setup.cfg/tox.ini file, e.g:

```
[flake8]
classmethod-decorators=declared_attr
staticmethod-decorators=staticdeco1,staticdeco2
```

I think B902 is great for detecting errors like this:

```
@classmethod
def foo(self):
    ...
```

However, I also find myself `# noqa:`ing it a bit too frequently. Here are some instances where I think it could be safely relaxed for everyone:

 - The first argument to `__new__` of classes that subclass `type` should probably be called `metacls`, not `cls`.
 - Allow `_cls` instead of `cls` (ditto for `metacls`). Rationale: this can be required when a method needs to take, or transparently pass through, a keyword argument with one of these names.
 - Consider allowing `subcls` for `__init_subclass__`, since that is actually significantly clearer. 

What do you think?

issue_title: `SecurityContext#delegated_creds` should be a property with documentation

issue_title: User level environment variables are ignored
issue_body:
I'm deploying a .NET Core application twice to the same server, once for QA, and once for Production. Currently I have each one of them using a different local user to run the IIS App Pool and have set the ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT variables to "qa" and "production" accordingly.

This doesn't appear to work, both sites run the production configuration which I understand is the default.

Things I've looked at so far:

1. I can log in as each of the users, and the variables are set correctly.
2. There is no system level env var set, though the user level vars are supposed to override it if there was.
3. I can see the processes running as the correct users in the task manager.
4. I have restarted the entire system after setting the env vars
5. It just appears that .NET is ignoring the environment variable.
6. I can set ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT at the system level, and both sites pick it up and run with it. Doing it without a system variable simply doesn't work and makes both sites default to production

Has anyone else managed to get user level env vars working with a .NET core deployment?

issue_title: Dockerfile is broken due to symlink bug in docker
issue_body:
Due to what appears to be a [bug in Docker](https://github.com/docker/docker/issues/17730) affecting either newer versions of Docker or perhaps related to certain storage configurations, the letsencrypt Dockerfile fails to build.

It fails due to an attempt to copy the `ubuntu.sh` symlink into a directory in the container image.

This is a problem that should be fixed upstream by Docker, but a simple workaround is to just specify the full destination filename. Pull request incoming.

issue_title: Ensure rename doesn't break third party plugins
issue_body:
There are a lot of third party plugins for Let's Encrypt (listed here: https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/using.html#plugins)
It's possible that they reference things in such a way that changing the name or package of LE will break their compatibility with the client - we should ensure that they are unbroken

issue_title: No Configurators (Authenticator and Installer) installed should suggest auth verb
issue_body:
If the user does not have any fully supported systems installed, the client should at least be informed about the 'auth' verb.

Location: after the pick_configurator call in cli.py
https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt/cli.py#L181

May be implemented with a display notification() mesage

issue_title: When installing a cert for a system that only has a *:80 model vhost, add ServerName / ServerAlias to the new vhost
issue_body: The failure to do this is one of the major reasons we hit the "please tell me which vhost to install on" dialog that underlies #1944 and other issues.
`NullInstaller` must not be available in `--help`, `letsencrypt plugins` or any other CLI menu. We want to preserve it though, to be able to run Boulder integration tests for `letsencrypt run`

issue_title: History file should handle multiline input properly
issue_body: Currently, multiline inputs are not roundtripped correctly by the history file, which breaks them over multiple separate lines.  This should be easy to fix, e.g. by dumping the history as JSON or even just python string repr's, which always fit in one line.

issue_title: should memv use eqv? rather than eq?
issue_body: That would take care of number/char issues.

issue_title: Inconsistent behaviour when dealing with dates close to year 0
issue_body:
I wrote the following simple test using the python-hypothesis library. It generates dates, renders them as strings, then unpacks them using `parser.parse()` and checks the date survived the trip:

``` python
from dateutil.parser import parse

@given(datetimes())
def test_round_trip(d):
    assert parse(str(d)) == d
```

If you try enough examples: this test fails:
- Pass in `d=datetime.datetime(4, 4, 1, 0, 0)` – i.e., April 1st in the year 4
- Casting to `str()`, this becomes `'0004-04-01 00:00:00'`
- When unpacked with `parse()`, it becomes `datetime.datetime(1, 4, 4, 0, 0)` – i.e., April 4th in the year 1. Mismatch!

The same problem occurs for all `datetime.datetime(M, M, 1, 0, 0)` for `1 < M <= 12`.

Given the four-digit year, I think this string is fairly unambiguous, but it seems to be getting confused somewhere, and treating the `-01` as the year. But [two-digit years are supposed](https://labix.org/python-dateutil#head-19e5d3331806e55ec2ba1b9e39458784cc0a0004) to be within 50 years of the current year:

> **Converting two digit years**
> 
> When a two digit year is found, it is processed considering the current year, so that the computed year is never more than 49 years after then current year, nor 50 years before the current year. In other words, if we are in year 2003, and the year 30 is found, it will be considered as 2030, but if the year 60 is found, it will be considered 1960.

So if it really is treating `-01` as the year, then based on this rule it should be returning `datetime.datetime(2001, 4, 4, 0, 0)`.

Hi there!
