See: https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/schema.py#L800

Decorator `validates_schema(pass_many=True)` does not actually pass the whole collection to the decorated method but still passes them one at time. The reason is at the linked line above (whenever `many==True` objects are passed one by one). Replacing that line with `if many and not pass_many` appears to fix the problem. Also notice the test for this functionality, [here](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/tests/test_decorators.py#L313), still passes because the dictionary passed has 2 items so `len(data) == 2` (even though it is only one data entry, not a collection).

For implementing `PATCH` handlers on REST endpoints, it would be useful to have a concept of partial deserialization.

This would mean ignoring missing required fields and default values for missing fields.

I know this sounds a bit weird, but it matches a standard CRUD endpoint fairly well - `POST` or `PUT` to that endpoint should use the full validation w/r/t required fields or defaults, but `PATCH` is intended to apply a partial update and only modify what was actually changed.

I can handle this in userspace by catching `ValidationError`s for missing fields, but I don't think I can do the same for ignoring default field values.

Here's the equivalent API in DRF: http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/serializers/#partial-updates. From my POV I think any `partial` arg (if you think it makes sense) would best be positioned as a named argument on `load`, though.

issue_title: Remove allow_blank parameter from String fields?
issue_body:
The `allow_blank` was added to address #76 . It is used to determine whether to allow the empty string as a valid value.

This parameter is technically redundant, since the same validation can be achieved using `validate.Length`.

```python
from marshamallow import fields, validate
not_blank = validate.Length(min=1, error='Field cannot be blank')
f = fields.Str(validate=not_blank)
f.deserialize('')  # => Error: Field may not be blank.
```

I propose to remove the `allow_blank` parameter, as it is unnecessary API.

issue_title: There is no "description" or otherwise helpful attribute of a field
issue_body:
Fields typically have some helpful text for a UI (be that graphical or command line) and it is surprising that marshmallow omits this. 

While it is possible to casually add arbitrary properties to a field definition, it seems that encouraging users to follow some standard might be a good idea. 

JSON Schema identifies "description" as a property to do this:
http://json-schema.org/latest/json-schema-validation.html#rfc.section.7.2

and, marshmallow-jsonschema implements this too:
https://github.com/fuhrysteve/marshmallow-jsonschema
https://github.com/fuhrysteve/marshmallow-jsonschema/blob/master/marshmallow_jsonschema/base.py#L139

Could this be included?

To determine the field class to be used for a SQLAlchemy column, we sometimes check its `python_type`. But some columns, e.g. Postgres `TSVECTOR` columns, don't have a `python_type` and raise a `NotImplementedError` on trying to access. Some possible fixes:

* Add `TSVECTOR` to `SQLA_TYPE_MAPPING`. This would kind of fix the immediate problem I'm running into, but what's the right marshmallow type to use? We could use `Str`, but that's not exactly right. Also, this doesn't help if other columns are missing `python_type`, which might or might not be the case.
* Allow developers to exclude unusual fields from model conversion, e.g. via the `Meta.exclude` option in marshmallow. This is more flexible but requires developers to understand that they can / should exclude columns that don't convert to fields.
* Don't raise an exception on failing to convert a column to a field. Possibly add a `strict` option to `fields_for_model`, such that `ModelConversionErrors` are only raised when `strict` is true. This is also flexible, but allows developers to silence potentially heterogeneous kinds of exceptions.

I'm submitting a patch for the second option, mostly so I can get some unrelated work done, but I think the third also makes sense, and it might not be bad to use both. What do you think @sloria?

     50             else:
     51                 # To show the full trace, we do nothing and let exception propagate
ImportError: cannot import name util

The [`create_from_cellranger()`](https://github.com/linnarsson-lab/loompy/blob/b91df787a57378f18a6269ae01985ab91d7a60a9/loompy/loompy.py#L890) function is documented to return a “LoomConnection to created loom file.”

It does not do this and actually returns only `None`. This is a problem as the caller first needs to infer the target file name in order to then `connect()` to it.

Possibly a better idea is to not return a connection, but the name of the created file. As the caller, I could then decide whether to `connect()` or not.

Inviting @satijalab, @mojaveazure, @pkharchenko @falexwolf @gioelelm @pl-ki @JobLeonard to discuss:

You're all invited because of your expertise writing single-cell pipelines. I think if we could all agree on a file format and some conventions, we could make our respective code much more interoperable. At a minimum, this would require each library to support reading and writing a common file format. I propose we go with Loom. :-)

But it will be extremely wasteful to write the whole dataset to file if you only added an attribute (e.g. a cluster ID). Better instead if each library just reads and writes the necessary attributes. How great wouldn't it be if we could easily do this (and vice versa in R):

```python
seurat = robjects.r['seurat']
pagoda = robjects.r['pagoda']
with loompy.connect("filename.loom") as ds:
    scanpy.api.pp.filter_cells(ds)
    cytograph.multiscale_knn(ds)
    seurat.cluster(ds)
    pagoda.diff_expression(ds)
```
(Of course, the code is made-up, but you get the idea)


To get there, we would need to agree on some conventions:

* Orientation of the dataset: rows versus columns
* Conventions for primary keys (maybe)
* Conventions for names of commonly used attributes

To get started, here's a proposal based on our current conventions, slightly cleaned up:

### Orientation

Rows are genes, columns are cells.

### Column attributes

`Cluster` (the cluster ID, int label from 0 to n_clusters)
`CellID` (string; unique, not really used, but assumed to be present)
`X`, `Y`, `Z` (embedding, e.g. tSNE or PCA; float; Z is optional)
`Valid` (0 or 1, indicating valid cells)

### Row attributes

`Gene` (gene symbol, string human-readable, not unique)
`Accession` (string, computer-readable, unique)
`Selected` (e.g. high-variance genes, 1 or 0)
`Valid` (passed some minimal criteria for being valid, 1 or 0)

### Graphs on cells

`KNN` (the knn graph)
`MKNN` (the mutual KNN graph)

### Graphs on genes

(none)

We also have conventions for layers, used e.g. for gene enrichment. For example, an aggregate Loom file would have one column per cluster instead of per cell, and would have layers such as `enrichment`, `enrichment_q` and `trinarization`, which give various metrics per gene and cluster. Not sure if it makes sense to standardize those, but please let us know what your pipelines might need along these lines.

**Note:** I'm opening the discussion here, but these are really intended to be conventions for *analysis libraries* and *visualization tools*, not Loom the file format. Loom itself is intended to be more general, so we will not impose on it e.g. that there will always be a `Gene` attribute. We may want to use loom for totally different omics datasets in the future.

Hi All!

Great to see a PV focused library with forecasting getting a lot of traction! Thanks and well done!

I'm currently working with a company that provides Irradiance and PV data (both forecasts and historical) derived (primarily) from satellite imagery and query-able from a simple HTTP API. We've had interest to use our data with this library.

issue_title: More efficient ways to use Z3 via its C API in the context of symbolic execution
issue_body:
This is a set of questions rather than an actual issue with Z3.

I currently have basic support for using Z3 via its C API implemented in the [KLEE symbolic execution engine](https://github.com/klee/klee). But I suspect how I'm using the API is sub-optimal so I'd like to discuss how Z3 is being currently used and how I can improve how I'm using it.

For reference you can find the code in KLEE to uses Z3 at

* https://github.com/klee/klee/blob/58f947302c9807b7f6bea2fcb2a7bb325f8dd1b2/lib/Solver/Z3Solver.cpp#L162
* https://github.com/klee/klee/blob/58f947302c9807b7f6bea2fcb2a7bb325f8dd1b2/lib/Solver/Z3Builder.cpp#L415

@ccadar @MartinNowack the answers here will likely be of interest to you.

So my questions currently are

* What solver should I be using? I'm currently using ``Z3_mk_simple_solver()`` but I noticed there are also ``Z3_mk_solver()``, ``Z3_mk_solver_for_logic()`` and ``Z3_mk_solver_from_tactic()`` functions. The documentation doesn't really make it clear what the differences are between the these calls. For example the documentation for ``Z3_mk_simple_solver()`` seems to talk about ``Z3_solver_get_model()`` and not much about what the solver being created actually does? In KLEE the logic is currently ``QF_ABV`` so would specifying this help?

* Currently in KLEE a new solver is created and destroyed for every query. Is this adding unnecessary overhead compared to having a persistent solver and just pushing and popping assertions? I was wary of doing that due to observing in the the past that the use of ``push`` and ``pop`` can drastically change the performance on some queries. Am I loosing anything (e.g. some sort of internal caching inside Z3) by not having a persistent solver? Note that in KLEE path exploration is not necessarily depth-first which means that queries given to Z3 are not guaranteed to be incremental which means that I might have to clear the entire stack of assertions every time a query is made (I suspect this might negate any advantages offered by having a persistent solver).

* Z3 has many parameters that control its behaviour. Are there any you would suggest setting in this context?

If any of these questions are unclear please let me know and I'll attempt to clarify.

issue_title: REST framework field doesn't work with model validation
issue_body:
Scenario: Want to add MinValueValidator to model money field.

A Decimal based minimum value doesn't work because on saving a model instance, djmoney complains it can't compare the decimal minimum value with the instance's Money object (understandably).

A Money based minimum value doesn't work because then REST framework field expects Decimal validators, so it complains it can't compare the Money minimum value with the REST request's Decimal value.

The only way to fix this is to repeat the Money field definition in the REST framework serializer class and make it use a second version of the MinValueValidator that is Decimal based.

I think we should be using Money based validation in the REST framework field instead of Decimal based. And make the REST Money field return Money objects rather than Decimal.

    c05331ac:   e3130004    tst r3, #4
    c05331b0:   0a000005    beq 0xc05331cc

`[r0, #90]` is a concrete location, with unknown value. Angr loads 0 into r3. This makes `beq 0xc05331cc` only has one successor.

How to configure it correctly, to change the value in r3, from 0 to a symbolic expression?

The situation is similar when dealing with STREX in ARM.

For example:

    strex   r1, r2, [r3]

Angr will directly set `r1` to `0`.

Hi,
I'm refactoring my code from `angr 6` to `angr 7`.
In `angr 6`, I used `Loader.add_object` to add my external object to the memory map.
But in `angr 7`, this interface has been moved.
So how can I use apis in `angr 7` to achieve my goal?

issue_title: Loading SEVIRI channel data which are not available in (native format) file
issue_body:
The MSG SEVIRI native format comes in one single file and may not include all channels.
Suppose the user try loading a channel which is not present in the file: At the moment the get_dataset method in the native_msg reader raises a KeyError. So, the issues is taken care of in principle, as it is possible to load other channels available in the file with the same scene instance. But, the KeyError handling is not propagated properly upwards in the code, resulting in a bit messy exception printouts, which is then repeated everytime a new channel is loaded.

As the HRIT data comes in segments and one file per channel this is not an issue there, but could potentially be an issue with other data with multiple channels in the same file.

We see two possible options:
1) Properly handle the KeyError upstream
2) When creating the scene object from the native format the header is read, and at that point missing and available datasets/channels are known. This information could be used before going into the channel loading. In that case the get_dataset would never need to return None or raise a KeyError.

Sauli Joro & Adam Dybbroe

issue_title: Better error reporting for unexpected 400 status
issue_body:
During development, tests will fail when the app returns Bad Request instead of the expected 200 or 201 code.  The error message from webtest only shows that 400 != 200, but with django forms or django-rest-framework validators, there is more information in the response body, but it’s not displayed.

Current workflow: change the test to have status=400 and print response.body.

Desired change: Define DjangoTestApp._check_status:

```python

    def _check_status(self, status, res):
        if status is not None and status != res.status_int:
            msg = 'Bad response: {} (not {})\n{}'.format(res.status, status, res.body)
            raise webtest.AppError(msg)
        super(DjangoTestApp, self)._check_status(status, res)
```

(Code adapted from https://stackoverflow.com/questions/13505778/finding-the-real-error-in-a-webtest-test-failure ; res.body shows all headers and response bytes, which always works compared to res.json for example)

issue_title: Calling a method with `user=None` is not equivalent to `set_user=None`
issue_body:
Hello!  I found a small inconsistency that’s mildly inconvenient.

Context: In a project where 90% of the views require authentication, I define a simple `app` fixture that calls `django_app.set_user('bill@example.ca')` and returns the app object.  For tests that check that the open views are indeed open, or that a specific view is indeed restricted (which is needed to make sure custom permissions work as intended with rest-framework for example), I can either use the base `django_app` fixture, or use my `app` and start the test with `app.set_user(None)` to remove `WEBTEST_USER` from the WSGI environment.

I found myself changing some tests to use a consistent style (pass `user=user2` or `user=None` everywhere instead of calling set_user in a few cases), and that did not reset the user.

Proposed fix: in `_update_environ`, use a sentinel object instead of None to determine if the `user` parameter was given or not; if it is None, remove `WEBTEST_USER`.

issue_title: Last character remains when spinner is stopped
issue_body:
When the spinner is stopped, the last spinner character remains on the screen.
Ideally, no spinner character should remain on the screen.

The current monitoring is a mire of threads, queues and locks which has grown to the point where it is extremely fragile. Long-term we have expressed a wish for a system which looks like the following:

> An external monitoring dæmon, written in Python 3 and using asyncio which, given a repository on disk will make all the calls to external services to check job status, download output (and do the submission?)

Of course, this doesn't solve the problem of having more than one agent needing to be able to write to a repository so we would still need lock files or make the dæmon act as a server too which all Ganga clients talk to (the dæmon would then maintain a queue of incoming new jobs and change requests).

Since we cannot rely on Python 3 yet, we cannot go straight to this system but an intermediate system which has the same interface characteristics could aid the transition.

The aim of this issue is to log our discussions on this topic to get an understanding of how a final system could look as well as the best way to progress towards it.

Hi all,

Apologies if this is a known bug but I keep getting:
```
Collecting ganga
  Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement ganga (from versions: )
No matching distribution found for ganga
```

from pip install ganga on multiple platforms. I don't know if this is known bug/limitation or not but these systems are not fedore/RHEL based/compatible so I don't know if this effects anything.

This is a minor detail as I can just clone and run Ganga for testing.

Thanks,

Rob

issue_title: Flask example needs to provide Facebook SDK version
issue_body:
### Version of the SDK being used
2.0.0

### Expected Behavior
Flask example should log into facebook.

### Actual Behavior
You get an error from Facebook about the version not being specified.
https://github.com/mobolic/facebook-sdk/blob/master/examples/flask/app/templates/base.html#L29

### Steps to Reproduce
Try to use flask example

The current approach with Basic Auth has very strong limitations. Mainly:
- It is hard to refresh the token (aka password) (every object permissions have to be modified before doing it)
- It is hard (if not impossible) to avoid users loosing their permissions [when rotating the hmac secret](https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/issues/691)

I propose that we make a simplistic `kinto.plugins.accounts` plugin that will: 
- Add a new CRUD resource (`/accounts`) where only the `create` permission is given
- The schema will be `id` (email regex?), `password` (PBKDF2, bcrypt or whatever) and additional metadata
- The object id would be forced to what is provided in basic auth header as user value
- Provide an `account` authentication policy using basic auth that will lookup in storage backend

When defining permissions on object it could just be `account:robert@patulacci.fr`.

To control who is allowed to create users, we would rely on the setting `kinto.account_create_principals` (like for buckets). When reaching the list of accounts, they would only their own object. Since they created the object, they would be allowed to modify and delete it.

We could deprecated the usage of the current basic auth.

I believe it would make authentication a bit more intuitive in Kinto, and would still be very easy to get started or deploy.

What do you think ?

issue_title: Usage of default bucket is costly
issue_body:
Because we want underlying bucket and collections objects to be created implicitly when using the `default` bucket, we knew we'd have some overhead. 

However, we implemented the implicit creation using Pyramid *subrequests*, and this is particularly inefficient.

If I disable the implicit creation, a local simulation load test with postgresql goes from `35 RPS` to `53 FPS` (~50% win).

If this default bucket is used in a `/batch`, it means two levels of cascaded subrequests are performed.

> This is problematic especially because default is the main bucket of kinto.js

I could get some major improvement by replacing the current subrequests with direct interaction on the storage backend:

```python
# views/buckets.py

def create_bucket(request, bucket_id):
    """Create a bucket if it doesn't exists."""
    bucket_put = (request.method.lower() == 'put' and
                  request.path.endswith('buckets/default'))

    if not bucket_put:
        request.matchdict['id'] = bucket_id
        service = Bucket(request)
        try:
            service.collection.create_record({'id': bucket_id})
        except exceptions.UnicityError:
            pass


def create_collection(request, bucket_id):
    subpath = request.matchdict.get('subpath')
    if subpath and subpath.startswith('collections/'):
        collection_id = subpath.split('/')[1]
        collection_put = (request.method.lower() == 'put' and
                          request.path.endswith(collection_id))
        if not collection_put:
            request.matchdict['bucket_id'] = bucket_id
            request.matchdict['id'] = collection_id
            service = Collection(request)
            try:
                service.collection.create_record({'id': collection_id})
            except exceptions.UnicityError:
                pass
```

But I now wonder why we don't just something like this:

```python
class Bucket(resource.ProtectedResource):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(Bucket, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        if self.record_id == 'default':
            digest = hmac_digest(hmac_secret, request.prefixed_userid)
            self.record_id = text_type(UUID(digest[:32]))
```
I think I asked already, but I can't remember. We should write it in the buckets code :)

```python
from django.contrib import admin

from oscar.core.loading import get_model

WeightBand = get_model('shipping', 'WeightBand')

    ]

    list_display = [
        'name',
        'description',

        'default_weight'
```

issue_title: Not running javascript when using proxy
issue_body:
i am using scrapy_splash to crawl pages that requires js to retrieve proper content.

Everything is fine when I use SplashRequest without proxy settings, but when I put in proxy settings, the javascript would not render, giving me prejavascript html content which do not have the data I need.

Does anyone know how to solve this? I am sure the proxy ip are not blacklisted.

When I try import SplashRequest error occurred:
>>> from scrapyjs import SplashRequest
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: cannot import name SplashRequest

but 
>>> import scrapyjs 
works.
---

Name: scrapyjs
Version: 0.2
Summary: JavaScript support for Scrapy using Splash
Home-page: https://github.com/scrapy-plugins/scrapy-splash
Author: Mikhail Korobov
Author-email: kmike84@gmail.com
License: BSD
Location: /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages

issue_title: Starts spark session when notebook starts
issue_body: Is there any possibilities to start spark session, when notebook starts? without magic comands?

issue_title: Create hiveContext only -- sqlContext is not needed
issue_body: "In addition to the basic SQLContext, you can also create a HiveContext, which provides a superset of the functionality provided by the basic SQLContext. Additional features include the ability to write queries using the more complete HiveQL parser, access to Hive UDFs, and the ability to read data from Hive tables. To use a HiveContext, you do not need to have an existing Hive setup, and all of the data sources available to a SQLContext are still available. HiveContext is only packaged separately to avoid including all of Hive’s dependencies in the default Spark build. If these dependencies are not a problem for your application then using HiveContext is recommended for the 1.3 release of Spark. "
Make the Alignak actions (acknowledge, downtime, re-check) default parameters configurable in the `settings.cfg` file

issue_title: Use webui components into another website
issue_body:
We can use webui components (command list, host list, livestate list, minemap...) on a website.

For that, we need:
* Pass the backend token in auth basic to webui call
* be able to ajax display a part of webui (like command list without headers, footers and menu)

issue_title: Error when getting a user in the backend
issue_body:

issue_title: RGBD stream stopping after 1hour with increasing ram requirement
issue_body:
### System Configuration
Please complete Your Configuration detail below. Refer to the BKC at [Configuration](../realsense_camera/README.md#configuration).

| Version               | Your Configuration   |
|:--------------------- |:---------------------|
| Operating System      | ubuntu 14.04                |
| Kernel                | 4.4.0-66-generic                |
| ROS                   | Indigo                  |
| ROS RealSense         | 1.8.0               |
| librealsense          | 1.12.1               |
| Camera Type-Firmware  | SR300 3.15.0.0            |

---

### Expected Behavior
The depth stream from topic /camera/depth_registered/points is obtained at all times. 

### Actual Behavior
The ram requirements of the process keeps increasing until it reaches to 95% of the total ram which in my case is 32GB. After eating up the ram the sensor stops publishing the topic. 
I have seen following messages after the node is shutdown

1. rs_wait_for_frames
2. [camera/camera_nodelet_manager-1] process has died [pid 10166, exit code -9, cmd /opt/ros/indigo/lib/nodelet/nodelet manager __name:=camera_nodelet_manager 
3. Without any message
I could receive more 100k frames in rviz before the node crashes.
I can't understand why the requirement of memory keeps increasing as the nodes don't store anything while processing input data. 
Kindly let me know what could be a way to run the sensor for longer durations.

### Steps to Reproduce
roslaunch realsense_camera sr300_nodelet_rgbd.launch

Thank you
Arpit

issue_title: Installing librealsense on Linux (but not librealsense2)
issue_body: Is there any easy way to do this? I have a pretty lengthy application written using librealsense (the original, not librealsense2), but I can't see an easy way to install it using apt, and going through each repository's history to find the last commit from SDK1 and then building from source seems like a bad idea

This would take in a structure and retain a list that contains the number of bonds (probably as fraction). For example, if a structure had 2 Li-O bonds and 3 Li-P bonds, it could return [0.4, 0.6] as the featurizer value and ["Li-O", "Li-P"] as the feature labels.

Some aspects to note:
* You probably want to allow any pymatgen NearNeighbor class to be used as the bond detection method. Some presets might help with usage
* If you are featurizing an entire data frame, you need the feature labels to match and be a concatenation of all the bonds in all the various structures in the data frame. Also some complications if you fit a model and then predict a structure for which there are no bonds in the training set. Some hashing tricks from text mining might help with this. Talk to AJ if you are implementing and this is confusing to you.

issue_title: Encourage open source contributions from the community
Repo and orphan delete can be executed cleanly after Lazy sync
issue_body:
1. Create a repository with on_demand sync plan
   
    pulp-admin rpm repo create --repo-id foo --feed https://repos.fedorapeople.org/repos/pulp/pulp/demo_repos/zoo/  --download-policy on_demand
   
   Assert
   1. Repo create successful
   2. It has Download Policy: on_demand
2. Synchronize the repository
    pulp-admin rpm repo sync run --repo-id foo
   
   Assert
   1. Sync succesful
   2. Repository has all the content
3. Delete the repo
   pulp-admin rpm repo delete --repo-id foo
   
   Assert
   1. Any consumer that has been bound to the repository has been unbound
   2. Repository delete is successful
   3. Any content that is part of the repository is an orphan state
      < pulp-admin orphan list>
4. Remove Orphans
   pulp-admin orphan remove --all
   
   Assert
   1. Orphan removal task successful
   2. All the orphaned content has been removed

All tests currently reside in `pulp_smash/tests/`. This works, but it doesn't scale well. How can a user find the kind of test they're looking for?

I'd recommend changing the layout from this:

```
pulp_smash
├── config
│   └── …
└── tests
    ├── test_content_applicability
    └── test_login
```

To this:

```
pulp_smash
├── config
│   └── …
└── tests
    ├── api_v1
    │   └── …
    ├── api_v2
    │   ├── test_content_applicability
    │   └── test_login
    └── cli
        └── …
```

issue_body:

For rpm

Assert

issue_title: [Feature request] Support for query complexity analysis
issue_body:
(I plan to implement it in the coming days if there's no strong objection/concern, this issue is mainly for discussion purpose)

Sangria has implemented a query complexity analysis, which is described here: http://sangria-graphql.org/learn/#query-complexity-analysis. We would like to implement something similar with Graphene.

We were able to implement a simple version (every node counts as 1) without modifying graphene code. But in order to implement a more complex and realistic version, some works need to be done inside Graphene.

The main problem we are facing is that we cannot figure out a way to add a custom property to nodes defined in our schema, something like:

```python
class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
  my_field = graphene.Field(
    MyType,
    description='some description',
    resolver=my_func,
    complexity=2,
  )
```

Even if we can overload `graphene.Field` to accept the complexity parameter in the constructor, we don't really get `Query` or `graphene.Field` back in the schema. Instead, we get `GraphQLField` or `GrapheneObjectType`, which already filtered that out.

There are a few different ways to make it possible:

1. Allow users to add custom properties to schema definitions (kind of like how we do `args` in `graphene.Field`), which will be kept in the schema. So the user could call `graphql.parse`, and then calculate the complexity with parsed AST and complexity info from the schema.

2. Add a `complexity` property to `graphene.Field`, which is default to `1` and could be either a number or a callable with parameter `args` that returns a number. Then, when we parse the query in `graphql-core`, pass in a `max_complexity` or something similar, calculate the complexity of the query, and reject the query if calculated complexity exceeds it.

Please do let me know if you have any concerns or suggestions. Thanks!

Maybe an edge case but I thought I'd ask anyway.

``` python
import graphene
from django.db import connection
from collections import namedtuple


class DatasourceType(graphene.ObjectType):

    id = graphene.String()
    COLUMN_A = graphene.String()
    COLUMN_B = graphene.String()
    COLUMN_C = graphene.String()


class QueryType(graphene.ObjectType):

    class Meta:
        name = 'Query'

    datasources = graphene.List(DatasourceType)

    def resolve_datasources(a, b, c, d):
        return get_datasources()


def get_db_rows(sql, args=None):
    cursor = connection.cursor()
    cursor.execute(sql, args)
    columns = [col[0] for col in cursor.description]
    RowType = namedtuple('Row', columns)
    data = [
        RowType(*row)
        for row in cursor.fetchall()]
    cursor.close()
    return data

    """)


schema = graphene.Schema(query=QueryType)
```

I am trying to handle recursive query with graphene-sqlachemy using a postgres database and cannot find a way to achieve what i want.

```

    __tablename__ = 'location'

        'children', uselist=True, cascade=False))
```

```

    class Meta:
        model = models.Location
        interfaces = (graphene.relay.Node, )

            .filter(models.Location.name == self.name)
            .cte(name='child_location', recursive=True))
        child_alias = child_location.alias('child')
        child_location = child_location.union_all(
```

issue_title: sys.prefix vs. other WD options for tests
issue_body:
Some tests were set up to use sys.prefix as the working directory because that is where the pip installation puts all the data files.  That was so that someone with the pip installation who has not downloaded from Github could run tests and make sure everything was working correctly on their machine, and also so that I could test the pip installation directly.  Using cwd + 'data_files' is going to break that functionality.  Here's an explanation about how that works with pip: [stack overflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/7934162/making-a-python-package-for-pip-with-some-data-files) 

However, using sys.prefix doesn't work correctly if you haven't installed using pip (or copied the data_files folder to sys.prefix + pmagpy_data_files).  Perhaps we can discuss how and where we want the tests to be able to run?  I like having the ability to directly test the pip installation, so maybe we could figure out how to get it to work both ways?

Also, quite a few tests are now failing for me.  'pmagpy_data_files' was not changed to 'data_files' everywhere, so a bunch of tests can't find test data.  Let's decide how we want to do things and get this fixed.    

@Caoimhinmg, @Swanson-Hysell ?

**Contexto**

Se está necesitando implementar la función `make_catalogs_backup()` para generar una copia de los datos y/o metadatos de uno o más catálogos en data.json, en un filesystem local.

La idea es que mediante una simple función, el usuario de pydatajson pueda generar una copia completa de un catálogo en data.json, descargándose todas las distribuciones y generando una copia de un catálogo en XLSX y JSON generando automáticamente un árbol de paths como el siguiente:

```
./catalog/{catalog_identifier}/catalog.xlsx
./catalog/{catalog_identifier}/data.json
./catalog/{catalog_identifier}/dataset/{dataset_identifier}/distribution/{distribution_identifier}/download/distribution-fileName.csv
```

**Propuesta**

Implementar un nuevo módulo `backup.py` para implementar estas funciones de backup siguiendo el patrón usual.

Implementar una función con la siguiente signature en ese módulo, e incorporarla al objeto `DataJson`:

```
make_catalogs_backup(catalogs=None,
                             local_catalogs_dir="my/local/path/",
                             copy_metadata=True, copy_data=True):
        """Realiza copia de los datos y metadatos de uno o más catálogos.

        Args:
            catalogs (list or dict): Lista de catálogos (elementos que pueden
                ser interpretados por DataJson como catálogos) o diccionario
                donde las keys se interpretan como los catalog_identifier:
                    {
                        "modernizacion": "http://infra.datos.gob.ar/catalog/modernizacion/data.json"
                    }
                Cuando es una lista, los ids se toman de catalog_identifer, y
                se ignoran los catálogos que no tengan catalog_identifier.
                Cuando se pasa un diccionario, los keys reemplazan a los
                catalog_identifier (estos no se leeen).
            local_catalogs_dir (str): Directorio local en el cual se va a crear
                la carpeta "catalog/..." con todos los catálogos.
            copy_metadata (bool): Si es verdadero, se generan los archivos
                data.json y catalog.xlsx.
            copy_data (bool): Si es verdadero, se descargan todas las
                distribuciones de todos los catálogos.

        Return:
            None
        """
```

La función permite crear una copia de datos, metadatos o ambos. Se puede usar en un sólo catálogo o en un conjunto. Siempre crea la carpeta `catalog` para iniciar el árbol de carpetas. No levanta errores si alguna carpeta no existe: crea las que necesita si no existen, o utiliza las que ya existen. Pisa archivos existentes pero no borra los anteriores. Si no se pasa un `local_catalogs_dir` se crea el _working directory_ actual (donde esté la terminal).

issue_title: Use Python GAPIC package name to determine Python gRPC package name
issue_body:
### What

The Python GAPIC package name is currently configured in the GAPIC config. The Python gRPC package name is determined by combining the prefix `google.cloud.grpc` with the proto package name. For consistency, it would be good to use the same package name structure for both GAPIC and gRPC; that is, any GAPIC package `{prefix}.gapic.{suffix}` will depend on the gRPC package `{prefix}.grpc.{suffix}`.

### Why

This avoids the verbosity of some proto package names while ensuring a consistent naming strategy across Python packages. See @bjwatson's comment in https://github.com/googleapis/api-client-staging/pull/154

### Concerns

The simplest implementation requires `artman` to read the GAPIC config to obtain the GAPIC package name and pass it to the gRPC package renaming task. I'm wary about introducing a dependency on the GAPIC config for a gRPC pipeline, since none currently exists, and such a dependency is counterintuitive.

An alternative is to move package name configuration to the artman config. This has the drawback that the package name must still be propagated to the GAPIC config (so it's duplicated across configs) and introduces the possibility of inconsistency if the GAPIC config is manually edited, but doesn't introduce any new dependencies to the gRPC pipeline. 

cc: @bjwatson
The package managers need to be supported now are maven and pypi.

For example, [this block][1] is no longer encountered during our Pub / Sub [system tests][2].

I'd guess it's because your code assumes the backend will throw `AbortionError` and similar, when it now throws `grpc._Rendezvous`.

becomes

python 3.6.0 :: Anaconda 4.3.1 (64-bit)
csvlook 1.0.2

Related to conversation in #737, i'd really like a memory efficient (and faster) version of schema generation. Let's start by describing how csvkit currently generates a schema (this is a bit simplified)

1. `csvsql` passes a file object to the [`from_csv` class method of Table](https://github.com/wireservice/csvkit/blob/e10d4a3caf5a88b5577bd2e8b69419c4024d3e9b/csvkit/utilities/csvsql.py#L124-L130)
2. `from_csv` reads the file as a csv an [saves all rows to a `rows` object](https://github.com/wireservice/csvkit/blob/e10d4a3caf5a88b5577bd2e8b69419c4024d3e9b/csvkit/utilities/csvsql.py#L124-L130)
3. `from_csv` passes `rows` to `Table.__init__`
3. `Table __init__` iterates through all rows to do [type inference](https://github.com/wireservice/agate/blob/master/agate/table/__init__.py#L109)
4. `Table __init__` iterates through all rows and [casts fields to appropriate type and saving result to a a `new_rows`](https://github.com/wireservice/agate/blob/master/agate/table/__init__.py#L116-L135). This effectively doubles the memory footprint until we exit from `__init__` and `rows` is garbage collected
5.  the `Table __init__` method exits and control is passed to the `csvsql` script
6.  `csvsql` calls `to_sql_create_statement` method is called on the Table object
7. `to_sql_create_statement` calls the `make_sql_table` method
8. if user is not using the `--no-constraints` flag on `csvsql` the `make_sql_table` [calculates precision and length constraints for every column](https://github.com/wireservice/agate-sql/blob/2be303738bbabea5471b0f6844763fd559571cf4/agatesql/table.py#L171-L187). This effectively doubles the memory footprint because the agate aggregation methods make calls `column.values` and `column.values_without_nulls` which create list objects of the values in a column. This methods are memoized, so these lists will remain in memory until the parent column object is garbage collected, which won't happen until csvsql exits.
9. `<agate-sql>` The column information is passed to `make_sql_column` which makes the appropriate sql schema entry for that column.

Here's an alternative flow that I would suggest for a streaming process:

1. csv reader is passed to a type inference that is similar to existing type inference. Exhaust all the rows. In addition to doing the type inference, we will also get information about max length and precision in this iteration
2. this information is passed to `make_sql_column' 

So, basically, bypassing creation of the Table object and expanding the functionality of the type tester.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Create example showing how hooks work
Bug in DataFrameMapper.get_names: unicode feature names
issue_body:
Hi,

When the features returned by a transformer (by `_get_feature_names`) are unicode strings containing non-ascii characters, `get_names` throws a `UnicodeEncodeError`. This is due to line 256 in `data frame_mapper.py`:
`return [name + '_' + str(o) for o in names]`
This assumes any object in `names` can be converted to string using `str`.
It would be much better to use something like `'%s_%s' % (name, o)`, which returns a unicode when needed. At the end, these strings become DataFrame column names, and DataFrames don't care about unicode indices.

issue_title: Resuming error handling functions after a signal
issue_body:
Despite me writing `certbot/error_handler.py` many months ago, it just surprised me. What surprised me is more or less documented below:

> Every registered function is attempted to be run to completion exactly once. If a registered function raises an exception, it is logged and the next function is called. If a (different) handled signal occurs while calling a registered function, it is attempted to be called again by the next signal handler.

I think not calling a registered function again if it just raised an exception makes sense. If it errored the first time, it's likely to error again. I'm not sure if calling the function again if it is interrupted by a signal, however, is the right behavior. Registered functions can change state and an interrupted function may leave something in an invalid state such that calling it again causes incorrect behavior.

For a real life example, `client.py` registers the method `_restart_and_rollback` with the `ErrorHandler`. This method rolls back one checkpoint and restarts the plugin. What if, however, a signal interrupts the method after rolling back the checkpoint. Calling it again will attempt to rollback another checkpoint which is incorrect.

I think we should call try and call a registered function at most once to avoid this problem.

via: https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/curl-refuses-to-accept-my-cert-saying-the-certificate-issuer-is-not-recognized/40917

Ubuntu 16.04

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):

Dev instructions (I was on commit 5d58a3d847f5a93b8eaaaea482063fae69d2b2d7 so this is unrelated to HTTP01 support in Nginx)

## I ran this command and it produced this output:

Starting with the default Nginx config on this system I ran:

```
sudo certbot -a webroot -w /var/www/html/ -d example.org -i nginx --no-redirect
sudo certbot -a webroot -w /var/www/html/ -d abc.example.org  -d def.example.org -i nginx --no-redirect
```

issue_title: "The requested apache plugin does not appear to be installed" on raspbian
issue_body:
I'm trying to use `certbot` on raspbian.

I managed to get it installed, I've also installed `libaugeas0` and `augeas-lenses`

`apache2` is installed either, with ssl enabled.

But when I run the command `sudo certbot --apache`, I get the following error:

`The requested apache plugin does not appear to be installed`

How can I fix that?

issue_title: Is there a way to write a short TLV?
issue_body:
I would like to write short TLV. Just TLV NDEF start flag (`0x03`), TLV NDEF message length, TLV NDEF message, TLV NDEF end flag (`0xFE`).

Currently, when I write a blank tag it fills with some extended TLV memory information. I am using NTAG213 and the tags are blank so I think it is coming from here:

https://github.com/nfcpy/nfcpy/blob/master/src/nfc/tag/tt2_nxp.py#L302

What is the recommended way to override this?

issue_title: Fix depreciated API use in tests/bench.py
issue_body:
> lz4.uncompress -> lz4.block.decompress

> lz4.compress -> lz4.block.compress

Otherwise stdout gets a line for each of the 200k loop iterations on each test. Didn't seem worth making a PR over, but annoying enough an upstream fix seemed appropriate.

https://github.com/lz4/lz4/releases

Add support for:

lz4hc : new high compression mode, by @inikep : levels 10-12 compress more (and slower), 12 is highest level

API : lz4frame : LZ4F_compressBound(0) provides upper bound of *flush() and *End() (#290, #280)

issue_title: Not crash on malformed messages and prepare for message versioning


you can easily notice that the message he received is in the old format with `channel` instead of `channel_identifier.


## Goal of the issue

Prepare for message versioning as outlined in #2161 and not crash on old/invalid messages.

## Task

- Since we may add fields to messages in the future and in order to be forward compatible we need to remove [this](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/encoding/format.py#L106) check from the UDP transport

- Make sure that we don't crash on malformed or old messages.

`pyethereum` isn't maintained properly any more and we should move away from it. I created a proof of concept on converting Raiden to use the web3 libraries. So far I got rid of most calls to our own RPC client (see [here](https://github.com/palango/raiden/commit/e49af6e6861856f93a47217c363e144b555513cd), and the [build](https://travis-ci.org/palango/raiden/builds/348175792)), but before I invest more time I'd like to know if you agree to the plan below.

I see the following two advantages in using web3 over the current situation:
- It's maintained and maintainers are responsible (I got some necessary patches merged in less than a day)
- We get a lot of goodies for free
  - [Caching](https://web3py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/middleware.html#cache)
  - [gas price strategies](https://web3py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/gas_price.html)
  - [Check if chain is synched](https://web3py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/middleware.html#stalecheck)
  - [Reconnection](https://web3py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/middleware.html#httprequestretry) (not in the form we have currently, but the basics are there)


The next steps would be:
1) Remove the `ContractProxy` and use `web3.contract.Contract` instead
2) Rework filtering to use the web3 APIs
3) Remove our own implementations of caching, reconnection and gas price strategies and replace them with the web3 implementations.
4) Remove as much usage of pyethereum APIs as possible
5) Rework testing to use eth-tester

The transfer tasks have too much state in the stack that need to be factored out, possibly into as class attributes. The attributes need to be saved as of #69 and restored once the process is starting up. 

Ideally this will remove the greenlet tasks for transfer in favor of state changes based on events.

In the case of a global remote mode (`-r`), there can be a need for a `local` object, which basically means I want this particular result to be transferred locally, or I would like to transfer this local resource to the remote server.

A lot can be done for this feature
1. For workflow output, append a DAG to the end. The DAG should be able to be embedded if it is in SVG format.
2. Provide links to document (e.g. paired_with to documentation about this function).
3. Syntax-hilight `${}`.
4. (done with option `--raw`) Add a link to the top right corner to link to the raw SoS file assuming that the raw file is in the same directory. This should be how our examples are hosted (raw sos + html).
5. A color legend to explain different types of lines.
6. A button to switch lineno so we do not need `--linenos` from command line.
7. For script output, display available workflows, something like

```
workflowname   step1 -> step 2 -> step 3
description of workflow
-------------
workflowname   step1 -> step 2 -> step 3
description of workflow
```

with steps link to their sections.

It seems to me that the default padding value for Pytorch is 0.0 as apparent here:

```
torch.nn.utils.rnn.pad_packed_sequence(sequence, batch_first=False, padding_value=0.0, total_length=None)
```

But, the padding value of Pytorch's text is rather 1.0:

https://github.com/pytorch/text/blob/7f0ed40229d680e64dc3fefbb39f2dccc255eb07/torchtext/data/field.py#L254

`self.pad_token` comes at the second place resulting in second in `itos` and hence padding value of 1.

I think having two different padding values is a bad idea? Even though I presume they won't really mix up together.

With torchtext v 0.2.1, It seems that the targets returned by the WikiText2 dataset correspond to the next window of `bptt_len` over the dataset. For a language modeling task, shouldn't the targets be simply the next word? 

concretely: `batch.text =  ['<eos>', '=', 'Valkyria', 'Chronicles', 'III']` and `batch.target =  ['=', 'Valkyria', 'Chronicles', 'III', '=']` and it is my understanding that `batch.target = ['=']` would be correct.


full testing code:
```python

from torchtext import data
from torchtext import datasets

TEXT = data.Field()

train_data, val_data, test_data = datasets.WikiText2.splits(TEXT)

TEXT.build_vocab(train_data, min_freq=10)

train_iter, val_iter, test_iter = data.BPTTIterator.splits(
            (train_data, val_data, test_data),
            batch_size=1, bptt_len=5, repeat=False)

batch = next(iter(train_iter))

def recover_text(vector):
    return [TEXT.vocab.itos[int(v)] for v in vector]

recover_text(batch.text.data.squeeze())  # >>> ['<eos>', '=', 'Valkyria', 'Chronicles', 'III']

recover_text(batch.target.data.squeeze()) # >>> ['=', 'Valkyria', 'Chronicles', 'III', '=']
```

Thanks for the awesome package.

-Austin

Today we have https://get.pulumi.com/install.sh and https://get.pulumi.com/install.ps1 as well as links on https://pulumi.io/install/ to download Pulumi.

It would be ideal if we had support for installing Pulumi via common package managers.  For example, it would be ideal if we had support for the following:

- [ ] Homebrew 
- [ ] Chocolatey 
- [ ] RPM/DEB packages

For all of these, it's probably simplest to start with our own repositories (e.g. a custom brew tap, seperate nuget feed Chocolatey, APT/RPM sources on get.pulumi.com you can add to your package manager) and then try to work with upstream distros to get it.

For DEB/RPM using something like `fpm` is likely going to be to start with, so we save ourselves from maintaining control and spec files.

Arch is probably also interesting, but I know less about about that packaging technology.  If there are any Arch fans here, please speak up and let us know what works best.

issue_title: Add gitlab ci for automatic deb package building

issue_body:
Ahem.
There's been a lot of discussion from us devs lately about Red's development workflow and how we can improve it. This issue aims to give a summary of that discussion.

# Introduction
Developing for Red in its current state can involve jumping through a lot of hoops and addressing many caveats in order to produce quality code and contributions. Part of this has to do with how we run tests, and another part is how we set up our development environment.

# Development Workflow Outline
Let's picture a developer first starting out contributing to Red. They needs to go through a couple of basic steps, as is the case with many python projects:
1. Fork and clone the repository
2. Install dependencies to a local virtual environment
3. Have some way of running his local development version of Red in said environment
4. Test their changes to verify they haven't broken everything
5. Open a pull request, subject to approval of the following conditions:
    5.1. The developers agree with your design and implementation
    5.2. A code review from the developers passes
    5.3. The Travis build succeeds

### So what's the matter with this?

Step (1) is all good.

Step (2) could be improved currently. One example for how we might do it right now are as follows:

    pip install git+https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py#egg=discord.py[voice]
    pip install -e .[docs,test,voice,mongo]

This didn't actually work with Red-Trivia<1.1, but luckily that will be history very soon thanks to #1635. In addition, upgrading dependencies involves repeating the same commands again with upgrade flags. I don't like big links.

Step (3) is pretty easy, simply run the bot like you normally would.

Step (4) involves installing pytest and running, but it's not just the usage of this which is lacking right now, but the actual coverage and extensiveness of our testing. For changes to docs, they also need to be built, which we don't really have a guide for. Moreover, style isn't enforced in any way.

Step (5) is all good, except that we should really be testing the stuff I described in step (4) on Travis too. This would help the devs a lot with code reviews.

Lastly, I'd like to note that although this is how I would recommend setting up a good environment to develop and test Red, this isn't the way Travis does it. Personally, I think that local testing and Travis should be standardised and unified.

# Making adjustments

#### Installation

~~Virtual environment tools can be handled by the developer in my opinion. End users will need hand holding when it comes to virtual environments, but it's not too hard for a developer to learn. Tools like pipenv, pyenv, virtualenv all work fine.~~

~~We can do less commands for installation by creating a 2nd pip requirements file, I'll call it `dev_install.txt`, so all we have to do to achieve step (2) is the following:~~

    pip install -r dev_install.txt

~~Pretty easy right? I think so too. Not only this, but it could be used exactly the same way to simplify the travis install script. This way, we would only need to modify this one file and it would update the development environment on both travis *and* local installations.~~

*Update:* We will be using [pipenv](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv) as an alternative to this approach. This manages the virtualenv *and* dependencies for development installation of the project. See #1638 and #1642 for reference.

#### Testing changes

Currently pytest works, but our code coverage is a pretty sad unfortunately. Moreover, it does nothing but unit testing; what if we want to enforce a style guide? What about building the documentation, how does the developer know how to test all of these things?

We could give them a guide for each of the individual tools used to test these things (pytest, sphinx, flake8, etc.) but I think that's too much writing and too much reading. Moreover, for any *new* testing methods we add, the developers would have to learn them. Luckily, there's a tool which is made specifically to help with this...

**[tox](https://github.com/tox-dev/tox)** is a great tool for integrating all of these tests together, and also running them on different versions of python in isolated virtual environments. The idea is that we configure tox to run particular tests, all the developer has to do is run the command `tox` in the project directory. The output can be configured to show the developer useful information about where the tests failed. 

Once again, Travis can be configured to use this same tool as we would be using locally, with similar benifits.

Installation of tox itself can be done as part of the `pip install -r dev_install.txt` described above.

## The Tools
So, I've talked about tox, which is the important part for your average developer. However we still need to discuss what we're actually testing with tox, since our pytest coverage at the moment is not enough.

From the discussion and from my own testing and research, I've come up with a draft list:
- [Black](https://github.com/ambv/black) and/or [flake8](https://github.com/PyCQA/flake8) for linting and stylechecking
- sphinx-build (OOTB with sphinx) for building docs and link checking
- [mypy](https://github.com/python/mypy) for static type checking

Separate issues can be created for discussing the exact configuration of the tools.

Also, we officially support all python versions from 3.5.1 to 3.6 latest. I think testing with the extremities of this range is sufficient (i.e. 3.5.1 and 3.6 latest).

issue_title: Write some docs explaining our thread pool philosophy
issue_body:
Basically: most systems use fixed-size thread pools for two reasons: (a) re-using threads is cheaper than spawning new ones, (b) you need some sort of global control over thread spawning to avoid accidentally overwhelming the system with `getaddrinfo` calls (or whatever). The problem with combining these though is that if you only have a single fixed-size thread pool for the whole system then that creates this global synchronization that can lead to deadlocks (see #6), so then you make multiple thread pools, but now you're doing a poor job of re-using threads...

So our philosophy is: there's an underlying run-in-thread primitive that might re-use threads for efficiency (currently it doesn't, but likely will sooner or later), but it acts as an *unbounded* thread pool. Then we layer the capacity control on top of that as a second layer, which allows for complex policies, multiple "virtual" thread pools that share the same underlying pool for efficiency, etc.

It's not *that* groundbreaking, but it's not how people usually think about these things so we need some conceptual-level documentation.

But it might still be useful to see that output if the test fails.

I played with pytest-catchlog, and while it does way more than we need (making assertions about the log messages emitted by the code being tested), the actual part where it captures the log messages seems ok:

```python
import logging

import pytest


@pytest.fixture
def log(request):
    return logging.getLogger(request.node.name)


def fake_thing():
    # imagine this being inside a module instead
    log = logging.getLogger('definitely_trio')
```

issue_title: [discussion] Rethinking resource cleanup for streams and files
issue_body:
`Stream`s, and presumably soon `Listener`s, implement the `trio.abc.AsyncResource` interface:
```python
def forceful_close(self):
    """Close immediately, even if this causes the shutdown to be "unclean"."""

async def graceful_close(self):
    """Close gracefully, blocking if necessary.

    SPECIAL CASE: if this is cancelled or otherwise raises an error, then the underlying resource is still
    closed (possibly in an "unclean" way).
    """
```

Then there's trio sockets which just have a sync `close` method, which is fine because socket closure isn't a blocking operation (outside of some really obscure and useless cases that you have to opt into... well, modulo [this silly bug](https://blogs.msdn.microsoft.com/winsdk/2013/10/09/udp-closesocket-takes-upto-5-seconds-to-return-in-disconnectremote-host-down-scenario-due-to-pending-data-to-send/)? [I think?](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1152046)), and the point of the `trio.socket` API is to directly expose all the quirky low-level details of sockets.

And finally there's async file objects (`trio.open_file`, `trio.wrap_file`), which have an *async* `close` method, because the underlying OS APIs just don't provide any truly non-blocking or forceful close for disk files. This `close` method has the same semantics as `graceful_close`: it blocks and executes a checkpoint, but if cancelled then it's still guaranteed to close the underlying object.

So I was thinking... giving streams and files different APIs for closing is confusing, especially since they're basically doing the same thing. So maybe we should make async file objects implement the `AsyncResource` API.

This would mean implementing a `forceful_close` that occasionally blocks the event loop for a little while (boo), but if you really need to get this thing closed immediately and you to do it from synchronous context, it will do that. And the `graceful_close` mechanism of course works fine.

But then I was thinking... given that implementation of `FileWrapper.forceful_close` and `FileWrapper.graceful_close`, you actually would never want to call `forceful_close`. The reason `forceful_close` exists is when you need to close something but can't afford to block indefinitely, e.g. because you have been cancelled. But if you think about it, really, the `AsyncResource` API provides *three* mechanisms for closing: there's the two above, and then there's:
```python
async def async_forceful_close(resource):
    with trio.cancel_scope() as cs:
        cs.cancel()
        await resource.graceful_close()
```

Because `async_forceful_close` runs in a cancelled context, it doesn't block (at least, not any more than the absolute minimum), but it's still a checkpoint and async-colored. And for `FileWrapper` this does the right thing, which sync `forceful_close` doesn't.

You can also imagine a resource that absolutely does need to block momentarily during even an unclean shutdown, like perhaps a database connection in an app where they decide as a matter of local policy that it should get that chance to shut down. Since `forceful_close` is sync-colored, it can't block at all no matter what (except by blocking the main loop).

And finally... there are not many contexts where it makes sense to call `forceful_close` directly. `AsyncResource` objects don't provide `with`, just `async with`. Internally, wrappers like `SSLStream.graceful_close` will call `self.transport_stream.forceful_close()` when they're on the path where things have failed and they need to do an unclean shutdown before re-raising, but they could use `async_forceful_close` too (with a tiny cost in efficiency, I guess). Our server helper will probably want to wrap each connection task in a last-ditch stream close operation:
```python
async def wrapped_connection_task(stream, afn):
    try:
        await afn(stream)
    finally:
        stream.forceful_close()
```
and this can't use `graceful_close` because it runs outside of any timeout control. But it could just as easily use something like the `async_forceful_close` incantation.

(Or maybe it should just let the descriptor leak, so that users see `ResourceWarning`?)

Anyway... it's really feeling like TOOWTDI is telling us to dump `forceful_close`, at least in its current form.

Concerns:
* I don't want to rename `graceful_close` to just `close`, that's confusing given Python's strong existing convention of `close` being a sync method
* The `with open_cancel_scope() as cs: cs.cancel(); stream.graceful_close()` incantation is **super obscure**.
* It is pretty handy when implementing things like `SSLStream.graceful_close` to have a nice way to say "welp I give up and this shutdown is going to be dirty" and propagate that to the underlying stream

Some options:

* Keep `AsyncResource.forceful_close`, make it async, give it a default implementation using the cancel-scope incantation, so from the caller's point of view it's parallel to `graceful_close`, and I guess subclasses can override if they want to for some tiny efficiency gains. (I guess we could even make both `forceful_close` and `graceful_close` have default implementations in terms of the other, and tell people to implement whichever one makes sense for their type? Unfortunately stock `abc.ABC` does not have a way to say "subclass must implement one or the other of these two methods". Also this is a lot of subclassy cleverness which I dislike.)

* Since `graceful_close` has to implement both types of behavior anyway, make it `graceful_close(can_block=True)`. Maybe change the name too, since `graceful_close(can_block=False)` is not actually graceful. But then we're still putting an extra burden on implementors: they have to implement any special handling for cancellation/errors, *and* they have to check for this bool, and they could potentially get out of sync.

* Provide a `trio.forceful_close` function that does the cancellation dance. It's not parallel to `graceful_close` (global function versus method), but most people never need to think about it anyway.

would like the module/CLI to work in environments that validate client certificates and have their own certificate authority certs published for server cert validation on the client side (2-way SSL). 

i'm happy to submit a PR on this but wanted to create an issue to track in case someone else has already looked at it. basically you just need to accept a string (and potentially convert to boolean for backward compat) for the `ssl_verify` attribute of the GitLab class which would pass the path of a CA cert list in PEM encoding to the `requests` module (as the `verify` parameter, as well as a `certs` parameter for `requests` which packs two values: the client cert and matching private key (both PEM encoded, unencrypted in the case of the private key).

In `requests`, one can set `verify` to the path of a CA certificate used to verify the the connection. This is really useful for CE instances when orgs run their own CA (ie, "self-signed").  This feature would allow me to not have to use ssl_verify=False.

issue_title: Question: does slack deal differently with incrementals
issue_body:
I noticed that I was not getting all messages from the channels I load. What I noticed, watching the terminal as my script runs to load data, is that with every p2o command I use (for Git, GitHub and Jira), it always states "Incremental from: date" after the line that it is feeding Ocean.

Now for my Slack command I am using:

p2o.py --enrich --index slack_raw --index-enrich slack -e http://localhost:9200 slack channelid -t myslacktoken

issue_title: Operator version updates are not necessary for BC-compatible changes
issue_body:
It was recently pointed out to me that what I have been telling people about operator versioning, and what Versioning.md says, do not line up.

From the spec:

> Once an operator is published, all implementations of that operator's domain, op_type, op_version MUST adhere to the signature and semantics of the operator at the time of publication. Any change of semantics implies a new operator, which MAY share a domain and op_type with another operator. This includes adding new behavior triggered only by previously unspecified inputs or attributes. These changes in semantics also MUST have a distinct operator id.

From what I have been saying:

> I'm not convinced that BC version bump here was necessary. We're simply adding a new attribute, and its default value (0) matches the old behavior, right? So this is a backwards-compatible change and no version update is necessary. (Of course, it is also semantically valid to do a version update; it just means that you're forcing producers to rev their targeted operator version to take advantage of the new version. Best not to make their life hard.)

I was under the impression we had agreed that BC-compatible changes wouldn't require revving the version (which is a somewhat disruptive thing to do, since no one gets the new behavior until they bump the operator spec version they're targeting.) I'm going to submit a PR to fix the wording here.

CC @donbox
